options.explanation: Only a null matrix can be symmetric as well as skew symmetric. In Symmetric Matrix A T = A, Skew Symmetric Matrix A T = -A, Given that the matrix is satisfying both the properties. Therefore, Equating the RHS we get A = -A i.e, 2A = 0. Therefore A = 0, which is a null matrix.

question_html:
Which of the given values of x and y make the pair of matrices equal
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3 x+7} & {5} \\ {y+1} & {2-3 x} \end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {y-2} \\ {8} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_text: Which of the given values of x and y make the pair of matrices equal {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3 x+7} & {5} \\ {y+1} & {2-3 x} \end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {y-2} \\ {8} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

options.explanation: In the given question: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3 x+7} & {5} \\ {y+1} & {2-3 x} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {y-2} \\ {8} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Since, the given matrices are equal, their corresponding elements are also equal. Comparing the corresponding element, we have: 3x + 7 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x=-\frac{7}{3}{/tex} Also, 5 = y – 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 7 Again, y + 1 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 7 And 2 – 3x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow ~x=-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} Therefore, on comparing the corresponding elements of the two matrices, we get different values of x, which is not possible. Thus, it is not possible to find the values of x and y for which the given matrices are equal.

explanation: In the given question: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3 x+7} & {5} \\ {y+1} & {2-3 x} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {y-2} \\ {8} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Since, the given matrices are equal, their corresponding elements are also equal. Comparing the corresponding element, we have: 3x + 7 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x=-\frac{7}{3}{/tex} Also, 5 = y – 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 7 Again, y + 1 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 7 And 2 – 3x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow ~x=-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} Therefore, on comparing the corresponding elements of the two matrices, we get different values of x, which is not possible. Thus, it is not possible to find the values of x and y for which the given matrices are equal.

question_text: The number of all scalar matrices of order 3, with each entry -1, 0 or 1, is:

options.explanation: A scalar matrix of order 3 has the form cI, where I is the identity matrix and c is a constant Given c can be -1, 0, or 1 , there are 3 distinct scalar matrices: -I, 0 and I. Therefore, the answer is 3.

question_html: If A is square matrix such that A2 = A, then (I + A)3 – 7 A is equal to
question_text: If A is square matrix such that A 2 = A, then (I + A) 3 – 7 A is equal to

options.explanation: Given that A 2 = A Calculating value of (I + A) 3 - 7A: {tex}(I+A)^3 - 7A=I^{3}+A^{3}+3 I^{2} A+3 I A^{2}-7 A{/tex} {tex}= \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{A}+3 \mathrm{A}+3 \mathrm{A}^{2}-7 \mathrm{A}\left(\mathrm{I}^{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{I} \text { and } \mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{A}\right){/tex} {tex}= I+A \cdot A+3 A+3 A-7 A\left(A^{2}=A\right){/tex} {tex}= I+A^{2}+3 A+3 A-7 A{/tex} = {tex}I +7A-7A{/tex} Hence, (I + A) 3 - 7A = {tex}I{/tex}

explanation: Given that A 2 = A Calculating value of (I + A) 3 - 7A: {tex}(I+A)^3 - 7A=I^{3}+A^{3}+3 I^{2} A+3 I A^{2}-7 A{/tex} {tex}= \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{A}+3 \mathrm{A}+3 \mathrm{A}^{2}-7 \mathrm{A}\left(\mathrm{I}^{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{I} \text { and } \mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{A}\right){/tex} {tex}= I+A \cdot A+3 A+3 A-7 A\left(A^{2}=A\right){/tex} {tex}= I+A^{2}+3 A+3 A-7 A{/tex} = {tex}I +7A-7A{/tex} Hence, (I + A) 3 - 7A = {tex}I{/tex}

options.explanation: Given the matrix {tex}A=\left[a_{i j}\right]{/tex} where {tex}a_{i j}=i-3 j{/tex} , we calculate: - {tex}a_{11}=-2{/tex} (true) - {tex}a_{12}+a_{21}=-6{/tex} (true) - {tex}a_{13}=-8{/tex} and {tex}a_{31}=0{/tex} , so {tex}-8>0{/tex} is false. Thus, the false statement is {tex} a_{13}>a_{31}{/tex}

question_html: If matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and A2 = kA, then the value of k is:
question_text: If matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and A 2 = kA, then the value of k is:
explanation: Now, {tex}A^2=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1+1 & -1-1 \\ -1-1 & 1+1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}A^2=2\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Given A 2 = kA {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]=k\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 2

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the value of x, if {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&5&1 \\ {15}&3&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
question_text: Find the value of x, if {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&5&1 \\ {15}&3&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&5&1 \\ {15}&3&2 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 2x + 15}&{3 + 5x + 3}&{2 + x + 2} \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {16 + 2x}&{5x + 6}&{x + 4} \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {16 + 2x + (5x + 6).2 + (x + 4)x} \right]_{1 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [16 + 2x + 10x + 12 + x 2 + 4x] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x 2 + 16x + 28] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x 2 + 2x + 14x + 28] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x + 14) = 0 {tex}\therefore {/tex} x = -2, -14
solution: We have, {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&5&1 \\ {15}&3&2 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 2x + 15}&{3 + 5x + 3}&{2 + x + 2} \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {16 + 2x}&{5x + 6}&{x + 4} \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {16 + 2x + (5x + 6).2 + (x + 4)x} \right]_{1 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [16 + 2x + 10x + 12 + x 2 + 4x] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x 2 + 16x + 28] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x 2 + 2x + 14x + 28] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x + 14) = 0 {tex}\therefore {/tex} x = -2, -14
solution_html:
We have, {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&5&1 \\ {15}&3&2 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 2x + 15}&{3 + 5x + 3}&{2 + x + 2} \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{/tex}{tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {16 + 2x}&{5x + 6}&{x + 4} \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {16 + 2x + (5x + 6).2 + (x + 4)x} \right]_{1 \times 1}} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [16 + 2x + 10x + 12 + x2 + 4x] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x2 + 16x + 28] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x2 + 2x + 14x + 28] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x + 14) = 0
{tex}\therefore {/tex} x = -2, -14

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the matrix X so that {tex}X\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
question_text: Find the matrix X so that {tex}X\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \;\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4b} \\ {c + 4d} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 5b} \\ {2c + 5d} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3a + 6b} \\ {3c + 6d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} On solving a + 4b = -7 and 2a + 5b = -8 & c + 4d = 2 and 2c + 5d = 4 we get a = 1, b = -2, c = 2, d = 0 {tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 2&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \;\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4b} \\ {c + 4d} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 5b} \\ {2c + 5d} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3a + 6b} \\ {3c + 6d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} On solving a + 4b = -7 and 2a + 5b = -8 & c + 4d = 2 and 2c + 5d = 4 we get a = 1, b = -2, c = 2, d = 0 {tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 2&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \;\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4b} \\ {c + 4d} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 5b} \\ {2c + 5d} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3a + 6b} \\ {3c + 6d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
On solving a + 4b = -7 and  2a + 5b = -8 & c + 4d = 2 and 2c + 5d = 4
we get a = 1, b = -2, c = 2, d = 0
{tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 2&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If A = {tex} =\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & \cot x \\ -\cot x & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, show that A' A-1 = {tex} \left[\begin{array}{rr} -\cos 2 x & -\sin 2 x \\ \sin 2 x & -\cos 2 x \end{array}\right]{/tex}.
question_text: If A = {tex} =\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & \cot x \\ -\cot x & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , show that A' A -1 = {tex} \left[\begin{array}{rr} -\cos 2 x & -\sin 2 x \\ \sin 2 x & -\cos 2 x \end{array}\right]{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} |A|=1+\cot ^2 x=\operatorname{cosec}^2 x {/tex} {tex} \operatorname{adj} A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot x \\ \operatorname{cotx} & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex} A^{-1}=\frac{\operatorname{adj} A}{|A|}={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec}^2 x}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot x \\ \cot x & 1 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot x \\ \cot x & 1 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} A^{\prime} A^{-1}={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec}^2 x}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1-\cot ^2 x & -2 \cot x \\ 2 \cot x & 1-\cot ^2 x \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} =\left[\begin{array}{cc} \sin ^2 x-\cos ^2 x & -2 \sin x \cos x \\ 2 \sin x \cos x & \sin ^2 x-\cos ^2 x \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} =\left[\begin{array}{cc} -\cos 2 x & -\sin 2 x \\ \sin 2 x & -\cos 2 x \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: {tex} |A|=1+\cot ^2 x=\operatorname{cosec}^2 x {/tex} {tex} \operatorname{adj} A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot x \\ \operatorname{cotx} & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex} A^{-1}=\frac{\operatorname{adj} A}{|A|}={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec}^2 x}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot x \\ \cot x & 1 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot x \\ \cot x & 1 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} A^{\prime} A^{-1}={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec}^2 x}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1-\cot ^2 x & -2 \cot x \\ 2 \cot x & 1-\cot ^2 x \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} =\left[\begin{array}{cc} \sin ^2 x-\cos ^2 x & -2 \sin x \cos x \\ 2 \sin x \cos x & \sin ^2 x-\cos ^2 x \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} =\left[\begin{array}{cc} -\cos 2 x & -\sin 2 x \\ \sin 2 x & -\cos 2 x \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} |A|=1+\cot ^2 x=\operatorname{cosec}^2 x {/tex}
{tex} \operatorname{adj} A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot x \\ \operatorname{cotx} & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex} A^{-1}=\frac{\operatorname{adj} A}{|A|}={/tex}{tex}\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec}^2 x}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot x \\ \cot x & 1 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot x \\ \cot x & 1 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} A^{\prime} A^{-1}={/tex}{tex}\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec}^2 x}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1-\cot ^2 x & -2 \cot x \\ 2 \cot x & 1-\cot ^2 x \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} =\left[\begin{array}{cc} \sin ^2 x-\cos ^2 x & -2 \sin x \cos x \\ 2 \sin x \cos x & \sin ^2 x-\cos ^2 x \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} =\left[\begin{array}{cc} -\cos 2 x & -\sin 2 x \\ \sin 2 x & -\cos 2 x \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} -1 & \mathrm{a} & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & \mathrm{x} \\ 3 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and A-1 = {tex} \left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ -8 & 7 & -5 \\ {~b} & {y} & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, find the value of (a + x) - (b + y).
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} -1 & \mathrm{a} & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & \mathrm{x} \\ 3 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and A -1 = {tex} \left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ -8 & 7 & -5 \\ {~b} & {y} & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , find the value of (a + x) - (b + y).
explanation: AA -1 = I {tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1 & a & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & x \\ 3 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ -8 & 7 & -5 \\ b & y & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]} {/tex} {tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1-8 a+2 b & 1+7 a+2 y & 5-5 a \\ -15+b x & 13+x y & 3 x-9 \\ -5+b & 4+y & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]} {/tex} -5 + b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 5, 5 - 5a = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 4 + y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -4, 3x - 9 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} (a + x) - (b + y) = (1 + 3) - (5 - 4) = 3
solution: AA -1 = I {tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1 & a & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & x \\ 3 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ -8 & 7 & -5 \\ b & y & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]} {/tex} {tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1-8 a+2 b & 1+7 a+2 y & 5-5 a \\ -15+b x & 13+x y & 3 x-9 \\ -5+b & 4+y & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]} {/tex} -5 + b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 5, 5 - 5a = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 4 + y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -4, 3x - 9 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} (a + x) - (b + y) = (1 + 3) - (5 - 4) = 3
solution_html:
AA-1 = I
{tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1 & a & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & x \\ 3 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ -8 & 7 & -5 \\ b & y & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]} {/tex}
{tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1-8 a+2 b & 1+7 a+2 y & 5-5 a \\ -15+b x & 13+x y & 3 x-9 \\ -5+b & 4+y & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]} {/tex}
-5 + b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 5, 5 - 5a = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1
4 + y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -4, 3x - 9 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (a + x) - (b + y) = (1 + 3) - (5 - 4) = 3

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find x, if {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{ - 5}&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
question_text: Find x, if {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{ - 5}&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{ - 5}&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x - 0 - 2}&{0 - 10 - 0}&{2x - 5 - 3} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x - 2}&{ - 10}&{2x - 8} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [(x - 2)x - 10(4) + (2x - 8)1] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x 2 - 2x - 40 + 2x - 8] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {{x^2} - 48} \right]_{1 \times 1}} = {\left[ 0 \right]_{1 \times 1}}{/tex} Equating corresponding entries, we have x 2 - 48 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 48 {tex}\Rightarrow x = \pm 4\sqrt 3{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{ - 5}&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x - 0 - 2}&{0 - 10 - 0}&{2x - 5 - 3} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x - 2}&{ - 10}&{2x - 8} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [(x - 2)x - 10(4) + (2x - 8)1] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x 2 - 2x - 40 + 2x - 8] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {{x^2} - 48} \right]_{1 \times 1}} = {\left[ 0 \right]_{1 \times 1}}{/tex} Equating corresponding entries, we have x 2 - 48 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 48 {tex}\Rightarrow x = \pm 4\sqrt 3{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{ - 5}&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x - 0 - 2}&{0 - 10 - 0}&{2x - 5 - 3} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x - 2}&{ - 10}&{2x - 8} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [(x - 2)x - 10(4) + (2x - 8)1] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x2 - 2x - 40 + 2x - 8] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {{x^2} - 48} \right]_{1 \times 1}} = {\left[ 0 \right]_{1 \times 1}}{/tex}
Equating corresponding entries, we have
x2 - 48 = 0
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 48
 {tex}\Rightarrow x = \pm 4\sqrt 3{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A shopkeeper sells 50 Chemistry, 60 Physics and 35 Maths books on day I and sells 40 Chemistry, 45 Physics and 50 Maths books on day II. If the selling price for each such subject book is ₹ 150 (Chemistry), ₹ 175 (Physics) and ₹ 180 (Maths), then find his total sale in two days, using matrix method. If cost price of all the books together is ₹ 35,000, what profit did he earn after the sale of two days?
question_text: A shopkeeper sells 50 Chemistry, 60 Physics and 35 Maths books on day I and sells 40 Chemistry, 45 Physics and 50 Maths books on day II. If the selling price for each such subject book is ₹ 150 (Chemistry), ₹ 175 (Physics) and ₹ 180 (Maths), then find his total sale in two days, using matrix method. If cost price of all the books together is ₹ 35,000, what profit did he earn after the sale of two days?
explanation: Let {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 50 & 60 & 35 \\ 40 & 45 & 50 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} & \text { Day I } \\ & \text { Day II } \end{aligned},{/tex} {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{l} 150 \\ 175 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]{/tex} be the day wise sale and the selling price per subject, matrices respectively. Total sales day wise {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 50 & 60 & 35 \\ 40 & 45 & 50 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 150 \\ 175 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 24,300 \\ 22,875 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} & \text { Day I } \\ & \text { Day II } \end{aligned}{/tex} Total sales in two days {tex}=₹ 24,300+₹ 22,875=₹ 47,175{/tex} {tex} \text { Profit }=₹ 47,175-₹ 35,000=₹ 12,175 \text {. } {/tex}
solution: Let {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 50 & 60 & 35 \\ 40 & 45 & 50 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} & \text { Day I } \\ & \text { Day II } \end{aligned},{/tex} {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{l} 150 \\ 175 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]{/tex} be the day wise sale and the selling price per subject, matrices respectively. Total sales day wise {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 50 & 60 & 35 \\ 40 & 45 & 50 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 150 \\ 175 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 24,300 \\ 22,875 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} & \text { Day I } \\ & \text { Day II } \end{aligned}{/tex} Total sales in two days {tex}=₹ 24,300+₹ 22,875=₹ 47,175{/tex} {tex} \text { Profit }=₹ 47,175-₹ 35,000=₹ 12,175 \text {. } {/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 50 & 60 & 35 \\ 40 & 45 & 50 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} & \text { Day I } \\ & \text { Day II } \end{aligned},{/tex} {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{l} 150 \\ 175 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]{/tex}be the day wise sale and the selling price per subject, matrices respectively.
Total sales day wise {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 50 & 60 & 35 \\ 40 & 45 & 50 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 150 \\ 175 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 24,300 \\ 22,875 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} & \text { Day I } \\ & \text { Day II } \end{aligned}{/tex}
Total sales in two days {tex}=₹ 24,300+₹ 22,875=₹ 47,175{/tex}
{tex} \text { Profit }=₹ 47,175-₹ 35,000=₹ 12,175 \text {. } {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If f({tex}\alpha{/tex}) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 0 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, prove that f({tex}\alpha{/tex}){tex} \cdot {/tex}f({tex}-\beta{/tex}) = f({tex}\alpha{/tex} - {tex}\beta{/tex})
question_text: If f( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 0 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , prove that f( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) {tex} \cdot {/tex} f( {tex}-\beta{/tex} ) = f( {tex}\alpha{/tex} - {tex}\beta{/tex} )
explanation: LHS = f( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) f( {tex}-\beta{/tex} ) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 0 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \beta & \sin \beta & 0 \\ -\sin \beta & \cos \beta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \alpha \cos \beta+\sin \alpha \sin \beta & \cos \alpha \sin \beta-\sin \alpha \cos \beta & 0 \\ \sin \alpha \cos \beta-\cos \alpha \sin \beta & \sin \alpha \sin \beta+\cos \alpha \cos \beta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos (\alpha-\beta) & -\sin (\alpha-\beta) & 0 \\ \sin (\alpha-\beta) & \cos (\alpha-\beta) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = f( {tex}\alpha{/tex} - {tex}\beta{/tex} ) = RHS
solution: LHS = f( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) f( {tex}-\beta{/tex} ) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 0 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \beta & \sin \beta & 0 \\ -\sin \beta & \cos \beta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \alpha \cos \beta+\sin \alpha \sin \beta & \cos \alpha \sin \beta-\sin \alpha \cos \beta & 0 \\ \sin \alpha \cos \beta-\cos \alpha \sin \beta & \sin \alpha \sin \beta+\cos \alpha \cos \beta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos (\alpha-\beta) & -\sin (\alpha-\beta) & 0 \\ \sin (\alpha-\beta) & \cos (\alpha-\beta) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = f( {tex}\alpha{/tex} - {tex}\beta{/tex} ) = RHS
solution_html:
LHS = f({tex}\alpha{/tex}) f({tex}-\beta{/tex}) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 0 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \beta & \sin \beta & 0 \\ -\sin \beta & \cos \beta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \alpha \cos \beta+\sin \alpha \sin \beta & \cos \alpha \sin \beta-\sin \alpha \cos \beta & 0 \\ \sin \alpha \cos \beta-\cos \alpha \sin \beta & \sin \alpha \sin \beta+\cos \alpha \cos \beta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos (\alpha-\beta) & -\sin (\alpha-\beta) & 0 \\ \sin (\alpha-\beta) & \cos (\alpha-\beta) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= f({tex}\alpha{/tex}-{tex}\beta{/tex}) = RHS

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Three friends Ravi, Raju and Rohit were doing buying and selling of stationery items in a market. The price of per dozen of pen, notebooks and toys are Rupees x, y and z respectively.
Ravi purchases 4 dozen of notebooks and sells 2 dozen of pens and 5 dozen of toys. Raju purchases 2 dozen of toy and sells 3 dozen of pens and 1 dozen of notebooks. Rohit purchases one dozen of pens and sells 3 dozen of notebooks and one dozen of toys.
In the process, Ravi, Raju and Rohit earn ₹1500, ₹100 and ₹400 respectively.

  1. Write the above information in terms of matrix Algebra. (1)
  2. What is the total price of one dozen of pens and one dozen of notebooks? (1)
  3. What is the sale amount of Ravi? (2)
    OR
    What is the amount of purchases and sales made by all three friends? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Three friends Ravi, Raju and Rohit were doing buying and selling of stationery items in a market. The price of per dozen of pen, notebooks and toys are Rupees x, y and z respectively. Ravi purchases 4 dozen of notebooks and sells 2 dozen of pens and 5 dozen of toys. Raju purchases 2 dozen of toy and sells 3 dozen of pens and 1 dozen of notebooks. Rohit purchases one dozen of pens and sells 3 dozen of notebooks and one dozen of toys. In the process, Ravi, Raju and Rohit earn ₹1500, ₹100 and ₹400 respectively. Write the above information in terms of matrix Algebra. (1) What is the total price of one dozen of pens and one dozen of notebooks? (1) What is the sale amount of Ravi? (2) OR What is the amount of purchases and sales made by all three friends? (2)
explanation: 2x - 4y + 5z = 1500 3x + y - 2z = 100 -x + 3y + z = 400 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -4 & 5 \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 1500 \\ 100 \\ 400 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -4 & 5 \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1500 \\ 100 \\ 400 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X = A -1 B ...(i) |A| = 2(1 + 6) + 4(3 - 2) + 5(9 + 1) = 68 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 co-factor matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -1 & 10 \\ 19 & 7 & -2 \\ 3 & 19 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 19 & 3 \\ -1 & 7 & 19 \\ 10 & -2 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|} \cdot a d j A{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 19 & 3 \\ -1 & 7 & 19 \\ 10 & -2 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex} From (i) {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 19 & 3 \\ -1 & 7 & 19 \\ 10 & -2 & 14 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 1500 \\ 100 \\ 400 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{c} 10500+1900+1200 \\ -1500+700+7600 \\ 15000-200+5600 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{c} 13600 \\ 6800 \\ 20400 \end{array}\right]{/tex} x = 200, y = 100 and z = 300 Total price of one dozen of pens and one dozen of notebooks = 200 + 100 = ₹300 The sale amount of Ravi = 2x + 5z = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 400 + 1500 = ₹1900 OR The amount of purchases made by all three friends 4y + 2z + x = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 + 200 = ₹1200 The amount of sales made by all three friends 2x + 5z + 3x + y + 3y + z = 5x + 4y + 6z = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = ₹3200
solution: 2x - 4y + 5z = 1500 3x + y - 2z = 100 -x + 3y + z = 400 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -4 & 5 \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 1500 \\ 100 \\ 400 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -4 & 5 \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1500 \\ 100 \\ 400 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X = A -1 B ...(i) |A| = 2(1 + 6) + 4(3 - 2) + 5(9 + 1) = 68 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 co-factor matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -1 & 10 \\ 19 & 7 & -2 \\ 3 & 19 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 19 & 3 \\ -1 & 7 & 19 \\ 10 & -2 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|} \cdot a d j A{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 19 & 3 \\ -1 & 7 & 19 \\ 10 & -2 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex} From (i) {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 19 & 3 \\ -1 & 7 & 19 \\ 10 & -2 & 14 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 1500 \\ 100 \\ 400 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{c} 10500+1900+1200 \\ -1500+700+7600 \\ 15000-200+5600 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{c} 13600 \\ 6800 \\ 20400 \end{array}\right]{/tex} x = 200, y = 100 and z = 300 Total price of one dozen of pens and one dozen of notebooks = 200 + 100 = ₹300 The sale amount of Ravi = 2x + 5z = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 400 + 1500 = ₹1900 OR The amount of purchases made by all three friends 4y + 2z + x = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 + 200 = ₹1200 The amount of sales made by all three friends 2x + 5z + 3x + y + 3y + z = 5x + 4y + 6z = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = ₹3200
solution_html:
1. 2x - 4y + 5z = 1500
    3x + y - 2z = 100
    -x + 3y + z = 400
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -4 & 5 \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 1500 \\ 100 \\ 400 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
  2. A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -4 & 5 \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex}, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1500 \\ 100 \\ 400 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    X = A-1B ...(i)
    |A| = 2(1 + 6) + 4(3 - 2) + 5(9 + 1) = 68 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
    co-factor matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -1 & 10 \\ 19 & 7 & -2 \\ 3 & 19 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 19 & 3 \\ -1 & 7 & 19 \\ 10 & -2 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|} \cdot a d j A{/tex}
    A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 19 & 3 \\ -1 & 7 & 19 \\ 10 & -2 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    From (i)
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 19 & 3 \\ -1 & 7 & 19 \\ 10 & -2 & 14 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 1500 \\ 100 \\ 400 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{c} 10500+1900+1200 \\ -1500+700+7600 \\ 15000-200+5600 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{68}\left[\begin{array}{c} 13600 \\ 6800 \\ 20400 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    x = 200, y = 100 and z = 300
    Total price of one dozen of pens and one dozen of notebooks = 200 + 100 = ₹300
  3. The sale amount of Ravi = 2x + 5z = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 400 + 1500 = ₹1900
    OR
    The amount of purchases made by all three friends
    4y + 2z + x = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 + 200 = ₹1200
    The amount of  sales made by all three friends
    2x + 5z + 3x + y + 3y + z = 5x + 4y + 6z = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = ₹3200

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
In a city, there are two factories A and B. Each factory produces sports clothes for boys and girls. There are three types of clothes produced in both the factories, type I, II and III. For boys, the number of units of types I, II, and III respectively are 80, 70, and 65 in factory A and 85, 65, and 72 are in factory B. For girls the number of units of types I, II, and III respectively are 80, 75, 90 in factory A and 50, 55, 80 are in factory B.

  1. Represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for both boys and girls and number of units of each type produced by factory B for both boys and girls in matrix form. (1)
  2. Find the total production of sports clothes of each type for boys. (1)
  3. Find the total production of sports clothes of each type for girls. (2)
    OR
    Let R be a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix that represent the total production of sports clothes of each type for boys and girls, then find the transpose of R. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: In a city, there are two factories A and B. Each factory produces sports clothes for boys and girls. There are three types of clothes produced in both the factories, type I, II and III. For boys, the number of units of types I, II, and III respectively are 80, 70, and 65 in factory A and 85, 65, and 72 are in factory B. For girls the number of units of types I, II, and III respectively are 80, 75, 90 in factory A and 50, 55, 80 are in factory B. Represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for both boys and girls and number of units of each type produced by factory B for both boys and girls in matrix form. (1) Find the total production of sports clothes of each type for boys. (1) Find the total production of sports clothes of each type for girls. (2) OR Let R be a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix that represent the total production of sports clothes of each type for boys and girls, then find the transpose of R. (2)
explanation: In factory A, number of units of types I, II and III for boys are 80, 70, 65 respectively and for girls number of units of types I, II and III are 80, 75,90 respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} P = {tex}\begin{array}{c}\text I\\\text{II}\\\text{III}\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Boys&Girls\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&80\\70&75\\65&90\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} In factory B, number of units of types I, II and III for boys are 85, 65, 72 respectively and for girls number of units of types I, II and III are 50, 55, 80 respectively. Q = {tex}\begin{array}{c}\text I\\\text{II}\\\text{III}\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Boys&Girls\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}85&50\\65&55\\72&80\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Let X be the matrix that represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for boys and Y be the matrix that represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B for boys. Then, X = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\text I&\text {II}&\text{III}\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&70&65\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} and Y = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\text I&\text {II}&\text{III}\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}85&65&72\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Now, required matrix = X + Y = [80 70 65] + [80 65 72] = [165 135 137] Let X be the matrix that represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for girls and Y be the matrix that represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B for girls. X = {tex}\mathop {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {80}&{75}&{90} \end{array}} \right]}\limits^{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} I&{II}&{III} \end{array}}{/tex} and Y = {tex}\mathop {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {50}&{55}&{80} \end{array}} \right]}\limits^{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} I&{II}&{III} \end{array}}{/tex} Required matrix = [80 75 90] + [50 55 80] = [130 130 137] OR Clearly, R = P + Q = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 80 & 80 \\ 70 & 75 \\ 65 & 90 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} 85 & 50 \\ 65 & 55 \\ 72 & 80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 165 & 130 \\ 135 & 130 \\ 137 & 170 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 165 & 135 & 137 \\ 130 & 130 & 170 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: In factory A, number of units of types I, II and III for boys are 80, 70, 65 respectively and for girls number of units of types I, II and III are 80, 75,90 respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} P = {tex}\begin{array}{c}\text I\\\text{II}\\\text{III}\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Boys&Girls\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&80\\70&75\\65&90\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} In factory B, number of units of types I, II and III for boys are 85, 65, 72 respectively and for girls number of units of types I, II and III are 50, 55, 80 respectively. Q = {tex}\begin{array}{c}\text I\\\text{II}\\\text{III}\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Boys&Girls\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}85&50\\65&55\\72&80\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Let X be the matrix that represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for boys and Y be the matrix that represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B for boys. Then, X = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\text I&\text {II}&\text{III}\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&70&65\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} and Y = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\text I&\text {II}&\text{III}\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}85&65&72\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Now, required matrix = X + Y = [80 70 65] + [80 65 72] = [165 135 137] Let X be the matrix that represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for girls and Y be the matrix that represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B for girls. X = {tex}\mathop {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {80}&{75}&{90} \end{array}} \right]}\limits^{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} I&{II}&{III} \end{array}}{/tex} and Y = {tex}\mathop {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {50}&{55}&{80} \end{array}} \right]}\limits^{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} I&{II}&{III} \end{array}}{/tex} Required matrix = [80 75 90] + [50 55 80] = [130 130 137] OR Clearly, R = P + Q = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 80 & 80 \\ 70 & 75 \\ 65 & 90 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} 85 & 50 \\ 65 & 55 \\ 72 & 80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 165 & 130 \\ 135 & 130 \\ 137 & 170 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 165 & 135 & 137 \\ 130 & 130 & 170 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
1. In factory A, number of units of types I, II and III for boys are 80, 70, 65 respectively and for girls number of units of types I, II and III are 80, 75,90 respectively.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} P = {tex}\begin{array}{c}\text I\\\text{II}\\\text{III}\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Boys&Girls\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&80\\70&75\\65&90\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} 
    In factory B, number of units of types I, II and III for boys are 85, 65, 72 respectively and for girls number of units of types I, II and III are 50, 55, 80 respectively.
    Q ={tex}\begin{array}{c}\text I\\\text{II}\\\text{III}\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Boys&Girls\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}85&50\\65&55\\72&80\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
  2. Let X be the matrix that represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for boys and Y be the matrix that represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B for boys.
    Then, X = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\text I&\text {II}&\text{III}\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&70&65\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} and Y = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\text I&\text {II}&\text{III}\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}85&65&72\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
    Now, required matrix = X + Y = [80 70 65] + [80 65 72]
    = [165 135 137]
  3. Let X be the matrix that represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for girls and Y be the matrix that represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B for girls.
    X = {tex}\mathop {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {80}&{75}&{90} \end{array}} \right]}\limits^{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} I&{II}&{III} \end{array}}{/tex} and Y = {tex}\mathop {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {50}&{55}&{80} \end{array}} \right]}\limits^{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} I&{II}&{III} \end{array}}{/tex}
    Required matrix = [80 75 90] + [50 55 80]
    = [130 130 137]
    OR
    Clearly, R = P + Q
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 80 & 80 \\ 70 & 75 \\ 65 & 90 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} 85 & 50 \\ 65 & 55 \\ 72 & 80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 165 & 130 \\ 135 & 130 \\ 137 & 170 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} R' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 165 & 135 & 137 \\ 130 & 130 & 170 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
In a city there are two factories A and B. Each factory produces sports clothes for boys and girls. There are three types of clothes produced in both the factories type I, type II and type III. For boys the number of units of types I, II and III respectively are 80, 70 and 65 in factory A and 85, 65 and 72 are in factory B. For girls the number of units of types I, II and III respectively are 80, 75, 90 in factory A and 50, 55, 80 are in factory B.

  1. Write the matrix P, if P represents the matrix of number of units of each type produced by factory A for both boys and girls. (1)
  2. Write the matrix Q, if Q represents the matrix of number of units of each type produced by factory B for both boys are girls. (1)
  3. Find the total production of sports clothes of each type for boys. (2)
    OR
    Find the total production of sports clothes of each type for girls. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: In a city there are two factories A and B. Each factory produces sports clothes for boys and girls. There are three types of clothes produced in both the factories type I, type II and type III. For boys the number of units of types I, II and III respectively are 80, 70 and 65 in factory A and 85, 65 and 72 are in factory B. For girls the number of units of types I, II and III respectively are 80, 75, 90 in factory A and 50, 55, 80 are in factory B. Write the matrix P, if P represents the matrix of number of units of each type produced by factory A for both boys and girls. (1) Write the matrix Q, if Q represents the matrix of number of units of each type produced by factory B for both boys are girls. (1) Find the total production of sports clothes of each type for boys. (2) OR Find the total production of sports clothes of each type for girls. (2)
explanation: In factory A, number of units of type I, II and III for boys are 80, 70, 65 respectively and for girls number of units of type I, II and III are 80, 75, 90 respectively. {tex}\therefore P = \overset{\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;Boys\;Girls}{\begin{array}{c}I\\II\\III\end{array}\left[\begin{array}{lc}80&80\\70&75\\65&90\end{array}\right]}{/tex} In factory B, number of units of type I, II and III for boys are 85, 65, 72 respectively and for girls number of units of type I, II and III are 50, 55, 80 respectively. {tex}\therefore Q=\overset{\;\;\;\;\;\;\;Boys\;Girls}{\begin{array}{c}I\\II\\III\end{array}\left[\begin{array}{lc}85&50\\65&55\\72&80\end{array}\right]}{/tex} Let matrix X represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for boys and matrix Y represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B for boys. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}X=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&70&65\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} {tex}Y=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}85&65&72\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Now, total production of sports clothes of each type for boys = X + Y = [80 70 65] + [85 65 72] = [165 135 137] OR For girls, let matrix S represents the number of units of each type produced by factory A and matrix T represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}S=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&75&90\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} {tex}T=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}50&55&80\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Now, required matrix = S + T = [80 75 90] + [50 55 80] = [130 130 170]
solution: In factory A, number of units of type I, II and III for boys are 80, 70, 65 respectively and for girls number of units of type I, II and III are 80, 75, 90 respectively. {tex}\therefore P = \overset{\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;Boys\;Girls}{\begin{array}{c}I\\II\\III\end{array}\left[\begin{array}{lc}80&80\\70&75\\65&90\end{array}\right]}{/tex} In factory B, number of units of type I, II and III for boys are 85, 65, 72 respectively and for girls number of units of type I, II and III are 50, 55, 80 respectively. {tex}\therefore Q=\overset{\;\;\;\;\;\;\;Boys\;Girls}{\begin{array}{c}I\\II\\III\end{array}\left[\begin{array}{lc}85&50\\65&55\\72&80\end{array}\right]}{/tex} Let matrix X represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for boys and matrix Y represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B for boys. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}X=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&70&65\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} {tex}Y=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}85&65&72\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Now, total production of sports clothes of each type for boys = X + Y = [80 70 65] + [85 65 72] = [165 135 137] OR For girls, let matrix S represents the number of units of each type produced by factory A and matrix T represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}S=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&75&90\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} {tex}T=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}50&55&80\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Now, required matrix = S + T = [80 75 90] + [50 55 80] = [130 130 170]
solution_html:
1. In factory A, number of units of type I, II and III for boys are 80, 70, 65 respectively and for girls number of units of type I, II and III are 80, 75, 90 respectively.
    {tex}\therefore P = \overset{\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;Boys\;Girls}{\begin{array}{c}I\\II\\III\end{array}\left[\begin{array}{lc}80&80\\70&75\\65&90\end{array}\right]}{/tex}

  2. In factory B, number of units of type I, II and III for boys are 85, 65, 72 respectively and for girls number of units of type I, II and III are 50, 55, 80 respectively.
    {tex}\therefore Q=\overset{\;\;\;\;\;\;\;Boys\;Girls}{\begin{array}{c}I\\II\\III\end{array}\left[\begin{array}{lc}85&50\\65&55\\72&80\end{array}\right]}{/tex}

  3. Let matrix X represent the number of units of each type produced by factory A for boys and matrix Y represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B for boys.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}X=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&70&65\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
    {tex}Y=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}85&65&72\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
    Now, total production of sports clothes of each type for boys = X + Y
    = [80 70 65] + [85 65 72]
    = [165 135 137]
    OR
    For girls, let matrix S represents the number of units of each type produced by factory A and matrix T represents the number of units of each type produced by factory B.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}S=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}80&75&90\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
    {tex}T=\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}\;\;I\;\;&II\;&III\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}50&55&80\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
    Now, required matrix = S + T
    = [80 75 90] + [50 55 80]
    = [130 130 170]

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Two farmers Ankit and Girish cultivate only three varieties of pulses namely Urad, Massor and Mung. The sale (in ₹) of these varieties of pulses by both the farmers in the month of September and October are given by the following matrices A and B.

September sales (in ₹):
A = {tex}\left( {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Urad}&{Masoor}&{Mung} \\ {10000}&{20000}&{30000} \\ {50000}&{30000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right)\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {Ankit} \\ {Girish} \end{array}{/tex}
October sales (in ₹):
A = {tex}\left( {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Urad}&{Masoor}&{Mung} \\ {5000}&{10000}&{6000} \\ {20000}&{30000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right)\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {Ankit} \\ {Girish} \end{array}{/tex}

  1. Find the combined sales of Masoor in September and October, for farmer Girish. (1)
  2. Find the combined sales of Urad in September and October, for farmer Ankit. (1)
  3. Find a decrease in sales from September to October. (2)
    OR
    If both the farmers receive 2% profit on gross sales, then compute the profit for each farmer and for each variety sold in October. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Two farmers Ankit and Girish cultivate only three varieties of pulses namely Urad, Massor and Mung. The sale (in ₹) of these varieties of pulses by both the farmers in the month of September and October are given by the following matrices A and B. September sales (in ₹): A = {tex}\left( {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Urad}&{Masoor}&{Mung} \\ {10000}&{20000}&{30000} \\ {50000}&{30000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right)\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {Ankit} \\ {Girish} \end{array}{/tex} October sales (in ₹): A = {tex}\left( {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Urad}&{Masoor}&{Mung} \\ {5000}&{10000}&{6000} \\ {20000}&{30000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right)\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {Ankit} \\ {Girish} \end{array}{/tex} Find the combined sales of Masoor in September and October, for farmer Girish. (1) Find the combined sales of Urad in September and October, for farmer Ankit. (1) Find a decrease in sales from September to October. (2) OR If both the farmers receive 2% profit on gross sales, then compute the profit for each farmer and for each variety sold in October. (2)
explanation: A + B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 10000 & 20000 & 30000 \\[0.3em] 50000 & 30000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} + {tex} \begin{bmatrix} 5000 & 10000 & 6000 \\[0.3em] 20000 & 10000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex} = \begin{bmatrix} 15000 & 30000 & 36000 \\[0.3em] 70000 & 40000 & 20000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} The combined sales of Masoor in September and October, for farmer Girish ₹40000. A + B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 10000 & 20000 & 30000 \\[0.3em] 50000 & 30000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} + {tex} \begin{bmatrix} 5000 & 10000 & 6000 \\[0.3em] 20000 & 10000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex} = \begin{bmatrix} 15000 & 30000 & 36000 \\[0.3em] 70000 & 40000 & 20000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} The combined sales of Urad in September and October, for farmer Ankit is ₹15000. A - B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 10,000 & 20,000 & 30,000 \\ 50,000 & 30,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5000 & 10,000 & 6000 \\ 20,000 & 10,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10,000-5000 & 20,000-10,000 & 30,000-6000 \\ 50,000-20,000 & 30,000-10,000 & 10,000-10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A - B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5000 & 10,000 & 24,000 \\ 30,000 & 20,000 & 0 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ankit } \\ \text { Girish } \end{gathered}{/tex} OR Profit = 2% {tex}\times{/tex} sales on october = {tex}\frac{2}{100} \times B{/tex} = {tex}0.02 \times\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5000 & 10,000 & 6000 \\ 20,000 & 10,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0.02 \times 5000 & 0.02 \times 10,000 & 0.02 \times 6000 \\ 0.02 \times 20,000 & 0.02 \times 10,000 & 0.02 \times 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 100 & 200 & 120 \\ 400 & 200 & 200 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ankit } \\ \text { Girish } \end{gathered}{/tex}
solution: A + B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 10000 & 20000 & 30000 \\[0.3em] 50000 & 30000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} + {tex} \begin{bmatrix} 5000 & 10000 & 6000 \\[0.3em] 20000 & 10000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex} = \begin{bmatrix} 15000 & 30000 & 36000 \\[0.3em] 70000 & 40000 & 20000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} The combined sales of Masoor in September and October, for farmer Girish ₹40000. A + B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 10000 & 20000 & 30000 \\[0.3em] 50000 & 30000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} + {tex} \begin{bmatrix} 5000 & 10000 & 6000 \\[0.3em] 20000 & 10000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex} = \begin{bmatrix} 15000 & 30000 & 36000 \\[0.3em] 70000 & 40000 & 20000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} The combined sales of Urad in September and October, for farmer Ankit is ₹15000. A - B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 10,000 & 20,000 & 30,000 \\ 50,000 & 30,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5000 & 10,000 & 6000 \\ 20,000 & 10,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10,000-5000 & 20,000-10,000 & 30,000-6000 \\ 50,000-20,000 & 30,000-10,000 & 10,000-10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A - B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5000 & 10,000 & 24,000 \\ 30,000 & 20,000 & 0 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ankit } \\ \text { Girish } \end{gathered}{/tex} OR Profit = 2% {tex}\times{/tex} sales on october = {tex}\frac{2}{100} \times B{/tex} = {tex}0.02 \times\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5000 & 10,000 & 6000 \\ 20,000 & 10,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0.02 \times 5000 & 0.02 \times 10,000 & 0.02 \times 6000 \\ 0.02 \times 20,000 & 0.02 \times 10,000 & 0.02 \times 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 100 & 200 & 120 \\ 400 & 200 & 200 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ankit } \\ \text { Girish } \end{gathered}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. A + B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 10000 & 20000 & 30000 \\[0.3em] 50000 & 30000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}+ {tex} \begin{bmatrix} 5000 & 10000 & 6000 \\[0.3em] 20000 & 10000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}  {tex} = \begin{bmatrix} 15000 & 30000 & 36000 \\[0.3em] 70000 & 40000 & 20000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} 
    The combined sales of Masoor in September and October, for farmer Girish ₹40000.
  2. A + B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 10000 & 20000 & 30000 \\[0.3em] 50000 & 30000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} +{tex} \begin{bmatrix} 5000 & 10000 & 6000 \\[0.3em] 20000 & 10000 & 10000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex} = \begin{bmatrix} 15000 & 30000 & 36000 \\[0.3em] 70000 & 40000 & 20000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} 
    The combined sales of Urad in September and October, for farmer Ankit is ₹15000.
  3. A - B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 10,000 & 20,000 & 30,000 \\ 50,000 & 30,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5000 & 10,000 & 6000 \\ 20,000 & 10,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10,000-5000 & 20,000-10,000 & 30,000-6000 \\ 50,000-20,000 & 30,000-10,000 & 10,000-10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    A - B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5000 & 10,000 & 24,000 \\ 30,000 & 20,000 & 0 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ankit } \\ \text { Girish } \end{gathered}{/tex}
    OR
    Profit = 2% {tex}\times{/tex} sales on october
    = {tex}\frac{2}{100} \times B{/tex} 
    = {tex}0.02 \times\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5000 & 10,000 & 6000 \\ 20,000 & 10,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0.02 \times 5000 & 0.02 \times 10,000 & 0.02 \times 6000 \\ 0.02 \times 20,000 & 0.02 \times 10,000 & 0.02 \times 10,000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 100 & 200 & 120 \\ 400 & 200 & 200 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ankit } \\ \text { Girish } \end{gathered}{/tex}

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
On her birthday, Seema decided to donate some money to the children of an orphanage home. If there were 8 children less, everyone would have got ₹10 more. However, if there were 16 children more, everyone would have got ₹10 less. Let the number of children be x and the amount distributed by Seema for one child be y (in ₹).

  1. Represent given information in matrix algebra. (1)
  2. Find the adjoint of Matrix containing information about of number of children and amount she paid? (1)
  3. Find the number of children who were given some money by Seema? (2)
    OR
    How much amount does Seema spend in distributing the money to all the students of the Orphanage? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: On her birthday, Seema decided to donate some money to the children of an orphanage home. If there were 8 children less, everyone would have got ₹10 more. However, if there were 16 children more, everyone would have got ₹10 less. Let the number of children be x and the amount distributed by Seema for one child be y (in ₹). Represent given information in matrix algebra. (1) Find the adjoint of Matrix containing information about of number of children and amount she paid? (1) Find the number of children who were given some money by Seema? (2) OR How much amount does Seema spend in distributing the money to all the students of the Orphanage? (2)
explanation: 5x - 4y = 40 5x - 8y = -80 {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{lllllllllllllll}5&{ - 4}\\5&{ - 8}\end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{lllllllllllllll}x\\y\end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{40}\\{ - 80}\end{array}} \right]{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & -4 \\ 5 & -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} |A| = -40 + 20 = -20 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 Cofactor matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & -5 \\ 4 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & -4 \\ 5 & -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} |A| = -40 + 20 = -20 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 Cofactor matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & -5 \\ 4 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X = A -1 B ...(i) A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|} \cdot a d j A{/tex} A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{-20} \cdot\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} From (i) {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-20} \cdot\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-20}\left[\begin{array}{l} -320-320 \\ -200-400 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 32 \\ 30 \end{array}\right]{/tex} x = 32 and y = 30 OR There are 32 Children, and each child is given ₹30. Total money spent by Seema = 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = ₹960 Hence Seema spends ₹960 in distributing the money to all the students of the Orphanage.
solution: 5x - 4y = 40 5x - 8y = -80 {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{lllllllllllllll}5&{ - 4}\\5&{ - 8}\end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{lllllllllllllll}x\\y\end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{40}\\{ - 80}\end{array}} \right]{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & -4 \\ 5 & -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} |A| = -40 + 20 = -20 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 Cofactor matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & -5 \\ 4 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & -4 \\ 5 & -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} |A| = -40 + 20 = -20 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 Cofactor matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & -5 \\ 4 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X = A -1 B ...(i) A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|} \cdot a d j A{/tex} A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{-20} \cdot\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} From (i) {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-20} \cdot\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-20}\left[\begin{array}{l} -320-320 \\ -200-400 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 32 \\ 30 \end{array}\right]{/tex} x = 32 and y = 30 OR There are 32 Children, and each child is given ₹30. Total money spent by Seema = 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = ₹960 Hence Seema spends ₹960 in distributing the money to all the students of the Orphanage.
solution_html:
1. 5x - 4y = 40
    5x - 8y = -80
    {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{lllllllllllllll}5&{ - 4}\\5&{ - 8}\end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{lllllllllllllll}x\\y\end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{40}\\{ - 80}\end{array}} \right]{/tex}
  2. A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & -4 \\ 5 & -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    |A| = -40 + 20 = -20 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
    Cofactor matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & -5 \\ 4 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
  3. A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & -4 \\ 5 & -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    |A| = -40 + 20 = -20 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
    Cofactor matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & -5 \\ 4 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    X = A-1 B ...(i)
    A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|} \cdot a d j A{/tex}
    A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{-20} \cdot\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    From (i)
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-20} \cdot\left[\begin{array}{ll} -8 & 4 \\ -5 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-20}\left[\begin{array}{l} -320-320 \\ -200-400 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 32 \\ 30 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    x = 32 and y = 30
    OR
    There are 32 Children, and each child is given ₹30.
    Total money spent by Seema = 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = ₹960
    Hence Seema spends ₹960 in distributing the money to all the students of the Orphanage.

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Three car dealers, say A, B and C, deals in three types of cars, namely Hatchback cars, Sedan cars, SUV cars. The sales figure of 2019 and 2020 showed that dealer A sold 120 Hatchback, 50 Sedan, 10 SUV cars in 2019 and 300 Hatchback, 150 Sedan, 20 SUV cars in 2020; dealer B sold 100 Hatchback, 30 Sedan, 5 SUV cars in 2019 and 200 Hatchback, 50 Sedan, 6 SUV cars in 2020; dealer C sold 90 Hatchback, 40 Sedan, 2 SUV cars in 2019 and 100 Hatchback, 60 Sedan, 5 SUV cars in 2020.

  1. Write the matrix summarizing sales data of 2019 and 2020. (1)
  2. Find the matrix summarizing sales data of 2020. (1)
  3. Find the total number of cars sold in two given years, by each dealer? (2)
    OR
    If each dealer receives a profit of ₹ 50000 on sale of a Hatchback, ₹100000 on sale of a Sedan and ₹200000 on sale of an SUV, then find the amount of profit received in the year 2020 by each dealer. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Three car dealers, say A, B and C, deals in three types of cars, namely Hatchback cars, Sedan cars, SUV cars. The sales figure of 2019 and 2020 showed that dealer A sold 120 Hatchback, 50 Sedan, 10 SUV cars in 2019 and 300 Hatchback, 150 Sedan, 20 SUV cars in 2020; dealer B sold 100 Hatchback, 30 Sedan, 5 SUV cars in 2019 and 200 Hatchback, 50 Sedan, 6 SUV cars in 2020; dealer C sold 90 Hatchback, 40 Sedan, 2 SUV cars in 2019 and 100 Hatchback, 60 Sedan, 5 SUV cars in 2020. Write the matrix summarizing sales data of 2019 and 2020. (1) Find the matrix summarizing sales data of 2020. (1) Find the total number of cars sold in two given years, by each dealer? (2) OR If each dealer receives a profit of ₹ 50000 on sale of a Hatchback, ₹100000 on sale of a Sedan and ₹200000 on sale of an SUV, then find the amount of profit received in the year 2020 by each dealer. (2)

Hatchbacks, 150 Sedans, 20 SUVs dealer B sold 200 Hatchbacks, 50 sedans, 6 SUVs dealer C sold 100 Hatchbacks, 60 sedans, 5 SUVs. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required matrix, say Q, is given by Q = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}300&150&20\\200&50&6\\100&60&5\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} In 2020, dealer A sold 300 Hatchback, 150 Sedan, 20 SUV dealer B sold 200 Hatchback, 50 sedan, 6 SUV dealer C sold 100 Hatchback, 60 sedan, 5 SUV. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required matrix, say Q, is given by Q = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}300&150&20\\200&50&6\\100&60&5\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Total number of cars sold in two given years, by each dealer, is given by P + Q = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}120+300&50+150&10+20\\100+200&30+50&5+6\\90+100&40+60&2+5\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}420&200&30\\300&80&11\\190&100&7\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} OR The amount of profit in 2020 received by each dealer is given by the matrix {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}300&150&20\\200&50&6\\100&60&5\end{bmatrix}}\begin{bmatrix}50000\\100000\\200000\end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{gathered} A \\ B \\ C \end{gathered}\left[\begin{array}{c} 15000000+15000000+4000000 \\ 10000000+5000000+1200000 \\ 5000000+6000000+1000000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}34000000\\16200000\\12000000\end{bmatrix}{/tex}

{tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}34000000\\16200000\\12000000\end{bmatrix}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}120&50&10\\100&30&5\\90&40&2\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} 
    In 2019, dealer A sold 120 Hatchbacks, 50 Sedans and 10 SUV; dealer B sold 100 Hatchbacks, 30 Sedans and 5 SUVs and dealer C sold 90 Hatchbacks, 40 Sedans and 2 SUVs.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required matrix, say P, is given by
    P = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}120&50&10\\100&30&5\\90&40&2\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} 
    In 2020, dealer A sold 300 Hatchbacks, 150 Sedans, 20 SUVs dealer B sold 200 Hatchbacks, 50 sedans, 6 SUVs dealer C sold 100 Hatchbacks, 60 sedans, 5 SUVs.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required matrix, say Q, is given by
    Q = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}300&150&20\\200&50&6\\100&60&5\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}300&150&20\\200&50&6\\100&60&5\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} 
    In 2020, dealer A sold 300 Hatchback, 150 Sedan, 20 SUV dealer B sold 200 Hatchback, 50 sedan, 6 SUV dealer C sold 100 Hatchback, 60 sedan, 5 SUV.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required matrix, say Q, is given by
    Q = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}300&150&20\\200&50&6\\100&60&5\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
  3. Total number of cars sold in two given years, by each dealer, is given by
    P + Q = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}120+300&50+150&10+20\\100+200&30+50&5+6\\90+100&40+60&2+5\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}420&200&30\\300&80&11\\190&100&7\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
    OR
    The amount of profit in 2020 received by each dealer is given by the matrix
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Hatchback&Sedan&SUV\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}300&150&20\\200&50&6\\100&60&5\end{bmatrix}}\begin{bmatrix}50000\\100000\\200000\end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    = {tex}\begin{gathered} A \\ B \\ C \end{gathered}\left[\begin{array}{c} 15000000+15000000+4000000 \\ 10000000+5000000+1200000 \\ 5000000+6000000+1000000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}34000000\\16200000\\12000000\end{bmatrix}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Three shopkeepers A, B and C go to a store to buy stationery. A purchase 12 dozen notebooks, 5 dozen pens and 6 dozen pencils. B purchases 10 dozen notebooks, 6 dozen pens and 7 dozen pencils. C purchases 11 dozen notebooks, 13 dozen pens and 8 dozen pencils. A notebook costs ₹40, a pen costs ₹12 and a pencil costs ₹3.

  1. How are the number of items purchased by shopkeepers A, B, and C represented in matrix form? (1)
  2. If X represents a matrix, and Y represents the matrix formed by the cost of each item, what does the product XY equal? (1)
  3. If A2 = A, then what is the value of (A + I)3 - 7A? (2)
    OR
    If A and B are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrices such that A2- B2 = (A - B) (A + B), then show that AB = BA (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Three shopkeepers A, B and C go to a store to buy stationery. A purchase 12 dozen notebooks, 5 dozen pens and 6 dozen pencils. B purchases 10 dozen notebooks, 6 dozen pens and 7 dozen pencils. C purchases 11 dozen notebooks, 13 dozen pens and 8 dozen pencils. A notebook costs ₹40, a pen costs ₹12 and a pencil costs ₹3. How are the number of items purchased by shopkeepers A, B, and C represented in matrix form? (1) If X represents a matrix, and Y represents the matrix formed by the cost of each item, what does the product XY equal? (1) If A 2 = A, then what is the value of (A + I) 3 - 7A? (2) OR If A and B are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrices such that A 2 - B 2 = (A - B) (A + B), then show that AB = BA (2)
explanation: Number of items purchased by shopkeepers A, B and C can be written in matrix form as X = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Notebooks&pens&pencils\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}144&60&72\\120&72&84\\132&156&96\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}{/tex} Since, Y = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}40\\12\\3\end{bmatrix}\begin{array}{c}Note\;book\\Pen\\Pencil\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} XY = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 144 & 60 & 72 \\ 120 & 72 & 84 \\ 132 & 156 & 96 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ 12 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 5760+720+216 \\ 4800+864+252 \\ 5280+1872+288 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 6696 \\ 5916 \\ 7440 \end{array}\right]{/tex} (A + I) 2 = A 2 + 2A + I = 3A + I {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (A + I) 3 = (3A + I) (A + I) = 3A 2 + 4A + I = 7A + I {tex}\therefore{/tex} (A + I) 3 - 7A = I OR A 2 - B 2 = (A - B) (A + B) = A 2 + AB - BA - B 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = BA
solution: Number of items purchased by shopkeepers A, B and C can be written in matrix form as X = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Notebooks&pens&pencils\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}144&60&72\\120&72&84\\132&156&96\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}{/tex} Since, Y = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}40\\12\\3\end{bmatrix}\begin{array}{c}Note\;book\\Pen\\Pencil\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} XY = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 144 & 60 & 72 \\ 120 & 72 & 84 \\ 132 & 156 & 96 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ 12 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 5760+720+216 \\ 4800+864+252 \\ 5280+1872+288 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 6696 \\ 5916 \\ 7440 \end{array}\right]{/tex} (A + I) 2 = A 2 + 2A + I = 3A + I {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (A + I) 3 = (3A + I) (A + I) = 3A 2 + 4A + I = 7A + I {tex}\therefore{/tex} (A + I) 3 - 7A = I OR A 2 - B 2 = (A - B) (A + B) = A 2 + AB - BA - B 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = BA
solution_html:
1. Number of items purchased by shopkeepers A, B and C can be written in matrix form as
    X = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Notebooks&pens&pencils\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}144&60&72\\120&72&84\\132&156&96\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}{/tex}

  2. Since, Y = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}40\\12\\3\end{bmatrix}\begin{array}{c}Note\;book\\Pen\\Pencil\end{array}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} XY = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 144 & 60 & 72 \\ 120 & 72 & 84 \\ 132 & 156 & 96 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ 12 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 5760+720+216 \\ 4800+864+252 \\ 5280+1872+288 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 6696 \\ 5916 \\ 7440 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

  3. (A + I)2 = A2 + 2A + I = 3A + I
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (A + I)3 = (3A + I) (A + I)
    = 3A2 + 4A + I = 7A + I
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} (A + I)3 - 7A = I
    OR
    A2 - B2 = (A - B) (A + B) = A2 + AB - BA - B2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = BA
