question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
A trust fund has ₹ 35000 that must be invested in two different types of bonds, say X and Y. The first bond pays 10% interest p.a. which will be given to an old age home and second one pays 8% interest p.a. which will be given to WWA (Women Welfare Association).
Let A be a 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix and B be a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 matrix, representing the investment and interest rate on each bond respectively.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions.

  1. If ₹ 15000 is invested in bond X, then what is the matrix representation of A and B? (1)
  2. If ₹ 15,000 is invested in bond X, how can we determine the total amount of interest received on both bonds? (1)
  3. How much is the investment in two bonds if the trust fund obtains an annual total interest of ₹3200? (2)
    OR
    What is the amount of investment in bond Y if the interest given to the old age home is ₹500? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A trust fund has ₹ 35000 that must be invested in two different types of bonds, say X and Y. The first bond pays 10% interest p.a. which will be given to an old age home and second one pays 8% interest p.a. which will be given to WWA (Women Welfare Association). Let A be a 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix and B be a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 matrix, representing the investment and interest rate on each bond respectively. Based on the above information, answer the following questions. If ₹ 15000 is invested in bond X, then what is the matrix representation of A and B? (1) If ₹ 15,000 is invested in bond X, how can we determine the total amount of interest received on both bonds? (1) How much is the investment in two bonds if the trust fund obtains an annual total interest of ₹3200? (2) OR What is the amount of investment in bond Y if the interest given to the old age home is ₹500? (2)
explanation: If ₹ 15000 is invested in bond X, then the amount invested in bond Y = ₹ (35000 - 15000) = ₹ 20000. A = Investment {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}X & Y \\ 15000 & 20000\end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\overset{Interest\;rate}{{}_Y^X\begin{bmatrix}10\%\\8\%\end{bmatrix}}\;=\overset{Interest\;rate}{{}_Y^X\begin{bmatrix}0.1\\0.08\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} The amount of interest received on each bond is given by AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 15000 & 20000 \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{c} 0.1 \\ 0.08 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [15000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 + 20000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.08] = [1500 + 1600] = 3100 Let ₹ x be invested in bond X and then ₹ (35000 - x) will be invested in bond Y. Now, total amount of interest is given by {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & 35000-x \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 0.1 \\ 0.08 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [0.1x + (35000 - x)0.08] But, it is given that total amount of interest = ₹ 3200 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.1x + 2800 - 0.08x = 3200 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.02x = 400 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 20000 Thus, ₹ 20000 invested in bond X and ₹ 35000 - ₹ 20000 = ₹ 15000 invested in bond Y. OR Let ₹ x invested in bond X, then we have {tex}x \times \frac{10}{100}{/tex} = 500 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5000 Thus, amount invested in bond X is ₹ 5000 and so investment in bond Y be ₹ (35000 - 5000) = ₹ 30000
solution: If ₹ 15000 is invested in bond X, then the amount invested in bond Y = ₹ (35000 - 15000) = ₹ 20000. A = Investment {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}X & Y \\ 15000 & 20000\end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\overset{Interest\;rate}{{}_Y^X\begin{bmatrix}10\%\\8\%\end{bmatrix}}\;=\overset{Interest\;rate}{{}_Y^X\begin{bmatrix}0.1\\0.08\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} The amount of interest received on each bond is given by AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 15000 & 20000 \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{c} 0.1 \\ 0.08 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [15000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 + 20000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.08] = [1500 + 1600] = 3100 Let ₹ x be invested in bond X and then ₹ (35000 - x) will be invested in bond Y. Now, total amount of interest is given by {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & 35000-x \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 0.1 \\ 0.08 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [0.1x + (35000 - x)0.08] But, it is given that total amount of interest = ₹ 3200 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.1x + 2800 - 0.08x = 3200 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.02x = 400 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 20000 Thus, ₹ 20000 invested in bond X and ₹ 35000 - ₹ 20000 = ₹ 15000 invested in bond Y. OR Let ₹ x invested in bond X, then we have {tex}x \times \frac{10}{100}{/tex} = 500 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5000 Thus, amount invested in bond X is ₹ 5000 and so investment in bond Y be ₹ (35000 - 5000) = ₹ 30000
solution_html:
1. If ₹ 15000 is invested in bond X, then the amount invested in bond Y = ₹ (35000 - 15000) = ₹ 20000.
    A = Investment {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}X & Y \\ 15000 & 20000\end{array}\right]{/tex}
    and B = {tex}\overset{Interest\;rate}{{}_Y^X\begin{bmatrix}10\%\\8\%\end{bmatrix}}\;=\overset{Interest\;rate}{{}_Y^X\begin{bmatrix}0.1\\0.08\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}

  2. The amount of interest received on each bond is given by
    AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 15000 & 20000 \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{c} 0.1 \\ 0.08 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = [15000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 + 20000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.08] = [1500 + 1600] = 3100

  3. Let ₹ x be invested in bond X and then ₹ (35000 - x) will be invested in bond Y.
    Now, total amount of interest is given by
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & 35000-x \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 0.1 \\ 0.08 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [0.1x + (35000 - x)0.08]
    But, it is given that total amount of interest = ₹ 3200
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.1x + 2800 - 0.08x = 3200
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.02x = 400 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 20000
    Thus, ₹ 20000 invested in bond X and ₹ 35000 - ₹ 20000 = ₹ 15000 invested in bond Y.
    OR
    Let ₹ x invested in bond X, then we have
    {tex}x \times \frac{10}{100}{/tex} = 500 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5000
    Thus, amount invested in bond X is ₹ 5000 and so investment in bond Y be ₹ (35000 - 5000) = ₹ 30000

question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A trust fund has ₹ 35000 that must be invested in two different types of bonds, say X and Y. Now, total amount of interest is given by [x 35000 - x] {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c}0.1 \\ 0.08\end{array}\right]{/tex} = [0.1x + (35000 - x) 0.08] But, it is given that total amount of interest = ₹3200 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.1x + 2800 - 0.08x = 3200 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.02 x = 400 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 20000 Thus, ₹20000 invested in bond X and ₹35000 - ₹20000 = ₹15000 invested in bond Y. Now, total amount of interest is given by [x 35000 - x] {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c}0.1 \\ 0.08\end{array}\right]{/tex} = [0.1x + (35000 - x) 0.08] But, it is given that total amount of interest = ₹3200 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.1x + 2800 - 0.08x = 3200 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.02 x = 400 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 20000 Thus, ₹20000 invested in bond X and ₹35000 - ₹20000 = ₹15000 invested in bond Y. 1. If ₹15000 is invested in bond X, then the amount invested in bond Y = ₹(35000 - 15000) = ₹20000
    Investment A = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}X&Y\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}15000&20000\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}; B = {tex}\overset{\text {Invest rate}}{\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}0.1\\0.08\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
A manufacture produces three stationery products Pencil, Eraser and Sharpener which he sells in two markets. Annual sales are indicated below:

Market Products (in numbers)
  Pencil Eraser Sharpener
I 10,000 2,000 18,000
II 6,000 20,000 8,000

If the unit Sale price of Pencil, Eraser and Sharpener are ₹2.50, ₹1.50 and ₹1.00 respectively,Based on the information given above, answer the following questions:

  1. What is the total revenue collected from Market-I? (1)
  2. What is the total revenue collected from Market-II? (1)
  3. What is the gross profit from both markets considering the unit costs of the three commodities as ₹2.00, ₹1.00, and 50 paise respectively? (2)
    OR
    If matrix A = {tex}\left[a_{i j}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}, where aij = 1, if i {tex}\neq{/tex} j and aij = 0 if i = j, then what is the value of A2? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A manufacture produces three stationery products Pencil, Eraser and Sharpener which he sells in two markets. Annual sales are indicated below: Market Products (in numbers) Pencil Eraser Sharpener I 10,000 2,000 18,000 II 6,000 20,000 8,000 If the unit Sale price of Pencil, Eraser and Sharpener are ₹2.50, ₹1.50 and ₹1.00 respectively,Based on the information given above, answer the following questions: What is the total revenue collected from Market-I? (1) What is the total revenue collected from Market-II? (1) What is the gross profit from both markets considering the unit costs of the three commodities as ₹2.00, ₹1.00, and 50 paise respectively? (2) OR If matrix A = {tex}\left[a_{i j}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} , where a ij = 1, if i {tex}\neq{/tex} j and a ij = 0 if i = j, then what is the value of A 2 ? (2)
explanation: Let A be the 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix representing the annual sales of products in two markets. {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} x & y & z \\ 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Let B be the column matrix representing the sale price of each unit of products x, y, z. {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, revenue = sale price {tex}\times{/tex} number of items sold = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 25000+3000+18000 \\ 15000+30000+8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, the revenue collected from Market I = ₹ 46000 Let A be the 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix representing the annual sales of products in two markets. {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} x & y & z \\ 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Let B be the column matrix representing the sale price of each unit of products x, y, z. {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, revenue = sale price {tex}\times{/tex} number of items sold = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 25000+3000+18000 \\ 15000+30000+8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} The revenue collected from Market II = ₹ 53000. Let C be the column matrix representing cost price of each unit of products x, y, z. Then, C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0.5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total cost in each market is given by AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0.5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 20000+2000+9000 \\ 12000+20000+4000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, Profit matrix = Revenue matrix - Cost matrix = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 15000 \\ 17000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, the gross profit from both the markets = ₹ 15000 + ₹ 17000 = ₹ 32000 OR We have, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = I
solution: Let A be the 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix representing the annual sales of products in two markets. {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} x & y & z \\ 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Let B be the column matrix representing the sale price of each unit of products x, y, z. {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, revenue = sale price {tex}\times{/tex} number of items sold = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 25000+3000+18000 \\ 15000+30000+8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, the revenue collected from Market I = ₹ 46000 Let A be the 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix representing the annual sales of products in two markets. {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} x & y & z \\ 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Let B be the column matrix representing the sale price of each unit of products x, y, z. {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, revenue = sale price {tex}\times{/tex} number of items sold = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 25000+3000+18000 \\ 15000+30000+8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} The revenue collected from Market II = ₹ 53000. Let C be the column matrix representing cost price of each unit of products x, y, z. Then, C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0.5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total cost in each market is given by AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0.5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 20000+2000+9000 \\ 12000+20000+4000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, Profit matrix = Revenue matrix - Cost matrix = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 15000 \\ 17000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, the gross profit from both the markets = ₹ 15000 + ₹ 17000 = ₹ 32000 OR We have, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = I
solution_html:
1. Let A be the 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix representing the annual sales of products in two markets.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} x & y & z \\ 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Let B be the column matrix representing the sale price of each unit of products x, y, z.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Now, revenue = sale price {tex}\times{/tex} number of items sold
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 25000+3000+18000 \\ 15000+30000+8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Therefore, the revenue collected from Market I = ₹ 46000

  2. Let A be the 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix representing the annual sales of products in two markets.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} x & y & z \\ 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Let B be the column matrix representing the sale price of each unit of products x, y, z.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Now, revenue = sale price {tex}\times{/tex} number of items sold
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 25000+3000+18000 \\ 15000+30000+8000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    The revenue collected from Market II = ₹ 53000.

  3. Let C be the column matrix representing cost price of each unit of products x, y, z.
    Then, C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0.5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total cost in each market is given by
    AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0.5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 20000+2000+9000 \\ 12000+20000+4000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Now, Profit matrix = Revenue matrix - Cost matrix
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 15000 \\ 17000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Therefore, the gross profit from both the markets = ₹ 15000 + ₹ 17000 = ₹ 32000
    OR
    We have, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} A2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = I
