question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Two farmers Shyam and Balwan Singh cultivate only three varieties of pulses namely Urad, Masoor and Mung. The sale (in ₹) of these varieties of pulses by both the farmers in the month of September and October are given by the following matrices A and B.
September sales (in ₹)
 
A = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}10000&20000&30000\\50000&30000&10000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex}
October sales (in ₹)
B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}5000&10000&6000\\20000&10000&10000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex}

  1. What were the combined sales of Masoor for farmer Balwan Singh in September and October? (1)
  2. What were the combined sales of Urad for farmer Shyam in September and October? (1)
  3. How much did the sales of Mung decrease from September to October for farmer Shyam? (2)
    OR
    How much profit did each farmer and each variety sold in October earn, considering they both received a 2% profit on their gross sales? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Two farmers Shyam and Balwan Singh cultivate only three varieties of pulses namely Urad, Masoor and Mung. The sale (in ₹) of these varieties of pulses by both the farmers in the month of September and October are given by the following matrices A and B. September sales (in ₹) A = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}10000&20000&30000\\50000&30000&10000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} October sales (in ₹) B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}5000&10000&6000\\20000&10000&10000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} What were the combined sales of Masoor for farmer Balwan Singh in September and October? (1) What were the combined sales of Urad for farmer Shyam in September and October? (1) How much did the sales of Mung decrease from September to October for farmer Shyam? (2) OR How much profit did each farmer and each variety sold in October earn, considering they both received a 2% profit on their gross sales? (2)
explanation: Combined sales in September and October for each farmer in each variety is given by A + B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}15000&30000&36000\\70000&40000&20000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} Combined sales of Masoor in September and October for farmer Balwan Singh = ₹ 40000 Combined sales in September and October for each farmer in each variety is given by A + B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}15000&30000&36000\\70000&40000&20000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} Combined sales of Urad in September and October for farmer Shyam = ₹ 15000 Change in sales from September to October is given by A - B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}5000&10000&24000\\30000&20000&0\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Decrease in sales of Mung from September to October for farmer Shyam = ₹ 24000. OR Required profit is given by 2% of B = {tex}\frac{2}{100} \times B{/tex} = 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} B = 0.02 {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}5000&10,000&6,000\\20,000&10,000&10,000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}100&200&120\\400&200&200\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} Thus, in October Shyam receives ₹ 100, ₹ 200 and ₹ 120 as profit in the sale of each variety of pulses, respectively and Balwan Singh receives a profit of ₹ 400, ₹ 200 and ₹ 200 in the sale of each variety of pulses respectively.
solution: Combined sales in September and October for each farmer in each variety is given by A + B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}15000&30000&36000\\70000&40000&20000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} Combined sales of Masoor in September and October for farmer Balwan Singh = ₹ 40000 Combined sales in September and October for each farmer in each variety is given by A + B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}15000&30000&36000\\70000&40000&20000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} Combined sales of Urad in September and October for farmer Shyam = ₹ 15000 Change in sales from September to October is given by A - B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}5000&10000&24000\\30000&20000&0\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Decrease in sales of Mung from September to October for farmer Shyam = ₹ 24000. OR Required profit is given by 2% of B = {tex}\frac{2}{100} \times B{/tex} = 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} B = 0.02 {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}5000&10,000&6,000\\20,000&10,000&10,000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}100&200&120\\400&200&200\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex} Thus, in October Shyam receives ₹ 100, ₹ 200 and ₹ 120 as profit in the sale of each variety of pulses, respectively and Balwan Singh receives a profit of ₹ 400, ₹ 200 and ₹ 200 in the sale of each variety of pulses respectively.
solution_html:
1. Combined sales in September and October for each farmer in each variety is given by
    A + B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}15000&30000&36000\\70000&40000&20000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex}
    Combined sales of Masoor in September and October for farmer Balwan Singh = ₹ 40000

  2. Combined sales in September and October for each farmer in each variety is given by
    A + B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}15000&30000&36000\\70000&40000&20000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex}
    Combined sales of Urad in September and October for farmer Shyam = ₹ 15000

  3. Change in sales from September to October is given by
    A - B = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}5000&10000&24000\\30000&20000&0\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Decrease in sales of Mung from September to October for farmer Shyam = ₹ 24000.
    OR
    Required profit is given by
    2% of B = {tex}\frac{2}{100} \times B{/tex} = 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} B

    = 0.02{tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}5000&10,000&6,000\\20,000&10,000&10,000\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex}

    = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}Urad&Masoor&Mung\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}100&200&120\\400&200&200\end{bmatrix}}\begin{array}{c}Shyam\\Balwan\;Singh\end{array}{/tex}
    Thus, in October Shyam receives ₹ 100, ₹ 200 and ₹ 120 as profit in the sale of each variety of pulses, respectively and Balwan Singh receives a profit of ₹ 400, ₹ 200 and ₹ 200 in the sale of each variety of pulses respectively.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
To promote the making of toilets for women, an organization tried to generate awareness through

  1. house calls
  2. emails and
  3. announcements.

The cost for each mode per attempt is given below:

  1. ₹ 50
  2. ₹ 20
  3. ₹ 40

The number of attempts made in the villages X, Y and Z are given below:

  (i) (ii) (iii)
X 400 300 100
Y 300 250 75
Z 500 400 150

Also, the chance of making of toilets corresponding to one attempt of given modes is

  1. 2%
  2. 4%
  3. 20%
  1. Find total number of toilets that can be expected after the promotion in village X. (1)
  2. Find the percentage of toilets that can be expected after the promotion in all the three-villages? (1)
  3. Find the cost incurred by the organization on village X. (2)
    OR
    Find the total cost incurred by the organization on for all the three villages? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: To promote the making of toilets for women, an organization tried to generate awareness through house calls emails and announcements. The cost for each mode per attempt is given below: ₹ 50 ₹ 20 ₹ 40 The number of attempts made in the villages X, Y and Z are given below: (i) (ii) (iii) X 400 300 100 Y 300 250 75 Z 500 400 150 Also, the chance of making of toilets corresponding to one attempt of given modes is 2% 4% 20% Find total number of toilets that can be expected after the promotion in village X. (1) Find the percentage of toilets that can be expected after the promotion in all the three-villages? (1) Find the cost incurred by the organization on village X. (2) OR Find the total cost incurred by the organization on for all the three villages? (2)
explanation: Total number of toilets that can be expected in each village is given by the following matrix. {tex}\begin{gathered} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{gathered}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 / 100 \\ 4 / 100 \\ 20 / 100 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}8&+&12&+&20\\6&+&10&+&15\\10&+&16&+&30\end{bmatrix}=\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}40\\31\\56\end{bmatrix}{/tex} X = 40, Y = 31, Z = 56 {tex}\begin{gathered} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{gathered}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 / 100 \\ 4 / 100 \\ 20 / 100 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}8&+&12&+&20\\6&+&10&+&15\\10&+&16&+&30\end{bmatrix}=\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}40\\31\\56\end{bmatrix}{/tex} Total attempt made in all the villages = 2475 Total number of toilets that can be expected after the promotion in all the three-villages = 40 + 31 + 56 = 127 The percentage of toilets that can be expected after the promotion in all the three-villages = {tex}\frac{{127}}{{2475}} \times 100 = 5.13\% {/tex} Let ₹A, ₹B and ₹C be the cost incurred by the organization for villages X, Y and Z respectively. Then A, B, C will be given by the following matrix equation. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 50 \\ 20 \\ 40 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 400 \times 50+300 \times 20+100 \times 40 \\ 300 \times 50+250 \times 20+75 \times 40 \\ 500 \times 50+400 \times 20+150 \times 40 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 20000+6000+4000 \\ 15000+5000+3000 \\ 25000+8000+6000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 30000 \\ 23000 \\ 39000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Cost is ₹30,000. OR {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 50 \\ 20 \\ 40 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 400 \times 50+300 \times 20+100 \times 40 \\ 300 \times 50+250 \times 20+75 \times 40 \\ 500 \times 50+400 \times 20+150 \times 40 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 20000+6000+4000 \\ 15000+5000+3000 \\ 25000+8000+6000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 30000 \\ 23000 \\ 39000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence total cost is = ₹92000
solution: Total number of toilets that can be expected in each village is given by the following matrix. {tex}\begin{gathered} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{gathered}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 / 100 \\ 4 / 100 \\ 20 / 100 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}8&+&12&+&20\\6&+&10&+&15\\10&+&16&+&30\end{bmatrix}=\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}40\\31\\56\end{bmatrix}{/tex} X = 40, Y = 31, Z = 56 {tex}\begin{gathered} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{gathered}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 / 100 \\ 4 / 100 \\ 20 / 100 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}8&+&12&+&20\\6&+&10&+&15\\10&+&16&+&30\end{bmatrix}=\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}40\\31\\56\end{bmatrix}{/tex} Total attempt made in all the villages = 2475 Total number of toilets that can be expected after the promotion in all the three-villages = 40 + 31 + 56 = 127 The percentage of toilets that can be expected after the promotion in all the three-villages = {tex}\frac{{127}}{{2475}} \times 100 = 5.13\% {/tex} Let ₹A, ₹B and ₹C be the cost incurred by the organization for villages X, Y and Z respectively. Then A, B, C will be given by the following matrix equation. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 50 \\ 20 \\ 40 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 400 \times 50+300 \times 20+100 \times 40 \\ 300 \times 50+250 \times 20+75 \times 40 \\ 500 \times 50+400 \times 20+150 \times 40 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 20000+6000+4000 \\ 15000+5000+3000 \\ 25000+8000+6000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 30000 \\ 23000 \\ 39000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Cost is ₹30,000. OR {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 50 \\ 20 \\ 40 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 400 \times 50+300 \times 20+100 \times 40 \\ 300 \times 50+250 \times 20+75 \times 40 \\ 500 \times 50+400 \times 20+150 \times 40 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 20000+6000+4000 \\ 15000+5000+3000 \\ 25000+8000+6000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 30000 \\ 23000 \\ 39000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence total cost is = ₹92000
solution_html:
1. Total number of toilets that can be expected in each village is given by the following matrix.
    {tex}\begin{gathered} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{gathered}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 / 100 \\ 4 / 100 \\ 20 / 100 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}8&+&12&+&20\\6&+&10&+&15\\10&+&16&+&30\end{bmatrix}=\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}40\\31\\56\end{bmatrix}{/tex} 
    X = 40, Y = 31, Z = 56
  2. {tex}\begin{gathered} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{gathered}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 / 100 \\ 4 / 100 \\ 20 / 100 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}8&+&12&+&20\\6&+&10&+&15\\10&+&16&+&30\end{bmatrix}=\begin{array}{c}X\\Y\\Z\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}40\\31\\56\end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    Total attempt made in all the villages = 2475
    Total number of toilets that can be expected after the promotion in all the three-villages = 40 + 31 + 56 = 127
    The percentage of toilets that can be expected after the promotion in all the three-villages = {tex}\frac{{127}}{{2475}} \times 100 = 5.13\% {/tex}
  3. Let ₹A, ₹B and ₹C be the cost incurred by the organization for villages X, Y and Z respectively. Then A, B, C will be given by the following matrix equation.
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 50 \\ 20 \\ 40 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 400 \times 50+300 \times 20+100 \times 40 \\ 300 \times 50+250 \times 20+75 \times 40 \\ 500 \times 50+400 \times 20+150 \times 40 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 20000+6000+4000 \\ 15000+5000+3000 \\ 25000+8000+6000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 30000 \\ 23000 \\ 39000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
    Cost is ₹30,000.
    OR
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 400 & 300 & 100 \\ 300 & 250 & 75 \\ 500 & 400 & 150 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 50 \\ 20 \\ 40 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 400 \times 50+300 \times 20+100 \times 40 \\ 300 \times 50+250 \times 20+75 \times 40 \\ 500 \times 50+400 \times 20+150 \times 40 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} A \\ B \\ C \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 20000+6000+4000 \\ 15000+5000+3000 \\ 25000+8000+6000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 30000 \\ 23000 \\ 39000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Hence total cost is = ₹92000

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Consider 2 families A and B. Suppose there are 4 men, 4 women and 4 children in family A and 2 men, 2 women and 2 children in family B. The recommended daily amount of calories is 2400 for a man, 1900 for a woman, 1800 for children and 45 grams of proteins for a man, 55 grams for a woman and 33 grams for children.
 

  1. Represent the requirement of calories and proteins for each person in matrix form. (1)
  2. Find the requirement of calories of family A and requirement of proteins of family B. (1)
  3. Represent the requirement of calories and proteins If each person increases the protein intake by 5% and decrease the calories by 5% in matrix form. (2)
    OR
    If A and B are two matrices such that AB = B and BA = A, then find A2 + B2  in terms of A and B. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Consider 2 families A and B. Suppose there are 4 men, 4 women and 4 children in family A and 2 men, 2 women and 2 children in family B. The recommended daily amount of calories is 2400 for a man, 1900 for a woman, 1800 for children and 45 grams of proteins for a man, 55 grams for a woman and 33 grams for children. Represent the requirement of calories and proteins for each person in matrix form. (1) Find the requirement of calories of family A and requirement of proteins of family B. (1) Represent the requirement of calories and proteins If each person increases the protein intake by 5% and decrease the calories by 5% in matrix form. (2) OR If A and B are two matrices such that AB = B and BA = A, then find A 2 + B 2 in terms of A and B. (2)
explanation: Let F be the matrix representing the number of family members and R be the matrix representing the requirement of calories and proteins for each person. Then F = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Family\;A\\Family\;B\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}& Men \ Women \ Children\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}4&4&4\\2&2&2\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} R = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Man\\woman\\Children\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Calories&Proteins\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}2400&45\\1900&55\\1800&33\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} The requirement of calories and proteins for each of the two families is given by the product matrix FR. FR = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2400 & 45 \\ 1900 & 55 \\ 1800 & 33 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 4(2400+1900+1800) & 4(45+55+33) \\ 2(2400+1900+1800) & 2(45+55+33) \end{array}\right]{/tex} FR = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{c}Calories\;Proteins \end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}24400&532\\12200&266\end{bmatrix}\begin{array}{c}Family\;A\\Family\;B\end{array}}{/tex} R' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2400-2400 \times 5 \% & 45+45 \times 5 \% \\ 1900-1900 \times 5 \% & 55+55 \times 5 \% \\ 1800-1800 \times 5 \% & 33+33 \times 5 \% \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2400-120 & 45+2.25 \\ 1900-95 & 55+2.75 \\ 1800-90 & 33+1.65 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R' = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Man\\Woman\\Children\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Calories&Proteins\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}2280&45.25\\1805&55.75\\1710&34.65\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} OR Since, AB = B ...(i) and BA = A ..(ii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 + B 2 = A {tex}\cdot{/tex} A + B {tex}\cdot{/tex} B = A(BA) + B(AB) [using (i) and (ii)] - (AB)A + (BA)B [Associative law] = BA + AB [using (i) and (ii)] = A + B
solution: Let F be the matrix representing the number of family members and R be the matrix representing the requirement of calories and proteins for each person. Then F = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Family\;A\\Family\;B\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}& Men \ Women \ Children\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}4&4&4\\2&2&2\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} R = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Man\\woman\\Children\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Calories&Proteins\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}2400&45\\1900&55\\1800&33\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} The requirement of calories and proteins for each of the two families is given by the product matrix FR. FR = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2400 & 45 \\ 1900 & 55 \\ 1800 & 33 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 4(2400+1900+1800) & 4(45+55+33) \\ 2(2400+1900+1800) & 2(45+55+33) \end{array}\right]{/tex} FR = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{c}Calories\;Proteins \end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}24400&532\\12200&266\end{bmatrix}\begin{array}{c}Family\;A\\Family\;B\end{array}}{/tex} R' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2400-2400 \times 5 \% & 45+45 \times 5 \% \\ 1900-1900 \times 5 \% & 55+55 \times 5 \% \\ 1800-1800 \times 5 \% & 33+33 \times 5 \% \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2400-120 & 45+2.25 \\ 1900-95 & 55+2.75 \\ 1800-90 & 33+1.65 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R' = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Man\\Woman\\Children\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Calories&Proteins\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}2280&45.25\\1805&55.75\\1710&34.65\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} OR Since, AB = B ...(i) and BA = A ..(ii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 + B 2 = A {tex}\cdot{/tex} A + B {tex}\cdot{/tex} B = A(BA) + B(AB) [using (i) and (ii)] - (AB)A + (BA)B [Associative law] = BA + AB [using (i) and (ii)] = A + B
solution_html:
1. Let F be the matrix representing the number of family members and R be the matrix representing the requirement of calories and proteins for each person. Then
    F = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Family\;A\\Family\;B\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}& Men \ Women \ Children\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}4&4&4\\2&2&2\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
    R = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Man\\woman\\Children\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Calories&Proteins\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}2400&45\\1900&55\\1800&33\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
  2. The requirement of calories and proteins for each of the two families is given by the product matrix FR.
    FR = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2400 & 45 \\ 1900 & 55 \\ 1800 & 33 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 4(2400+1900+1800) & 4(45+55+33) \\ 2(2400+1900+1800) & 2(45+55+33) \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    FR = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{c}Calories\;Proteins \end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}24400&532\\12200&266\end{bmatrix}\begin{array}{c}Family\;A\\Family\;B\end{array}}{/tex}
  3. R' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2400-2400 \times 5 \% & 45+45 \times 5 \% \\ 1900-1900 \times 5 \% & 55+55 \times 5 \% \\ 1800-1800 \times 5 \% & 33+33 \times 5 \% \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2400-120 & 45+2.25 \\ 1900-95 & 55+2.75 \\ 1800-90 & 33+1.65 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R' = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Man\\Woman\\Children\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Calories&Proteins\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}2280&45.25\\1805&55.75\\1710&34.65\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
    OR
    Since, AB = B ...(i) and BA = A ..(ii)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} A2 + B2 = A{tex}\cdot{/tex}A + B{tex}\cdot{/tex}B
    = A(BA) + B(AB) [using (i) and (ii)]
    - (AB)A + (BA)B [Associative law]
    = BA + AB [using (i) and (ii)]
    = A + B

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Three schools A, B and C organized a mela for collecting funds for helping the rehabilitation of flood victims. They sold handmade fans, mats, and plates from recycled material at a cost of ₹ 25, ₹ 100 and ₹ 50 each. The number of articles sold by school A, B, C are given below.

Article School A B C
Fans 40 25 35
Mats 50 40 50
Plates 20 30 40
  1. Represent the sale of handmade fans, mats and plates by three schools A, B and C and the sale prices (in ₹) of given products per unit, in matrix form. (1)
  2. Find the funds collected by school A, B and C by selling the given articles. (1)
  3. If they increase the cost price of each unit by 20%, then write the matrix representing new price. (2)
    OR
    Find the total funds collected for the required purpose after 20% hike in price. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Three schools A, B and C organized a mela for collecting funds for helping the rehabilitation of flood victims. They sold handmade fans, mats, and plates from recycled material at a cost of ₹ 25, ₹ 100 and ₹ 50 each. The number of articles sold by school A, B, C are given below. Article School A B C Fans 40 25 35 Mats 50 40 50 Plates 20 30 40 Represent the sale of handmade fans, mats and plates by three schools A, B and C and the sale prices (in ₹) of given products per unit, in matrix form. (1) Find the funds collected by school A, B and C by selling the given articles. (1) If they increase the cost price of each unit by 20%, then write the matrix representing new price. (2) OR Find the total funds collected for the required purpose after 20% hike in price. (2)
explanation: P = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}&&Fans&Mats&Plates\end{array}}{\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}40&50&20\\25&40&30\\35&50&40\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Q = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{25}\\{100}\\{50}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}{/tex} Clearly, total funds collected by each school is given by the matrix PQ = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 40 & 50 & 20 \\ 25 & 40 & 30 \\ 35 & 50 & 40 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 25 \\ 100 \\ 50 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1000+5000+1000 \\ 625+4000+1500 \\ 875+5000+2000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 7000 \\ 6125 \\ 7875 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Funds collected by school A is ₹7000. Funds collected by school B is ₹6125. Funds collected by school C is ₹7875. {tex}\begin{array}{l}New\,\,\,price\,\,matrix\,\,Q = 20\% \times \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{25}\\{100}\\{50}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}\\ \Rightarrow Q = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{25 + 25 \times 0.20}\\{100 + 100 \times 0.20}\\{50 + 50 \times 0.20}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}\\Q = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{30}\\{120}\\{60}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}\end{array}{/tex} New price matrix Q = {tex}20 \% \times\left[\begin{array}{c} 25 \\ 100 \\ 50 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Fans } \\ \text { Mats } \\ \text { Plates } \end{gathered}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 25+25 \times 0.20 \\ 100+100 \times 0.20 \\ 50+50 \times 0.20 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} &\text { Fans } \\ &\text { Mats } \\ &\text { Plates } \end{aligned}{/tex} Q = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 30 \\ 120 \\ 60 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} &\text { Fans } \\ &\text { Mats } \\ &\text { Plates } \end{aligned}{/tex} OR PQ = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 40 & 50 & 20 \\ 25 & 40 & 30 \\ 35 & 50 & 40 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 30 \\ 120 \\ 60 \end{array}\right]{/tex} PQ = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1200+6000+1200 \\ 750+4800+1800 \\ 1050+6000+2400 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 8400 \\ 7350 \\ 9450 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Total fund collected = 8400 + 7350 + 9450 = ₹25,200
solution: P = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}&&Fans&Mats&Plates\end{array}}{\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}40&50&20\\25&40&30\\35&50&40\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} Q = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{25}\\{100}\\{50}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}{/tex} Clearly, total funds collected by each school is given by the matrix PQ = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 40 & 50 & 20 \\ 25 & 40 & 30 \\ 35 & 50 & 40 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 25 \\ 100 \\ 50 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1000+5000+1000 \\ 625+4000+1500 \\ 875+5000+2000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 7000 \\ 6125 \\ 7875 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Funds collected by school A is ₹7000. Funds collected by school B is ₹6125. Funds collected by school C is ₹7875. {tex}\begin{array}{l}New\,\,\,price\,\,matrix\,\,Q = 20\% \times \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{25}\\{100}\\{50}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}\\ \Rightarrow Q = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{25 + 25 \times 0.20}\\{100 + 100 \times 0.20}\\{50 + 50 \times 0.20}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}\\Q = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{30}\\{120}\\{60}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}\end{array}{/tex} New price matrix Q = {tex}20 \% \times\left[\begin{array}{c} 25 \\ 100 \\ 50 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Fans } \\ \text { Mats } \\ \text { Plates } \end{gathered}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 25+25 \times 0.20 \\ 100+100 \times 0.20 \\ 50+50 \times 0.20 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} &\text { Fans } \\ &\text { Mats } \\ &\text { Plates } \end{aligned}{/tex} Q = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 30 \\ 120 \\ 60 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} &\text { Fans } \\ &\text { Mats } \\ &\text { Plates } \end{aligned}{/tex} OR PQ = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 40 & 50 & 20 \\ 25 & 40 & 30 \\ 35 & 50 & 40 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 30 \\ 120 \\ 60 \end{array}\right]{/tex} PQ = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1200+6000+1200 \\ 750+4800+1800 \\ 1050+6000+2400 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 8400 \\ 7350 \\ 9450 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Total fund collected = 8400 + 7350 + 9450 = ₹25,200
solution_html:
1.  P = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}&&Fans&Mats&Plates\end{array}}{\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}40&50&20\\25&40&30\\35&50&40\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}
    Q = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{25}\\{100}\\{50}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}{/tex}
  2. Clearly, total funds collected by each school is given by the matrix
    PQ = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 40 & 50 & 20 \\ 25 & 40 & 30 \\ 35 & 50 & 40 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 25 \\ 100 \\ 50 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1000+5000+1000 \\ 625+4000+1500 \\ 875+5000+2000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 7000 \\ 6125 \\ 7875 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Funds collected by school A is ₹7000.
    Funds collected by school B is ₹6125.
    Funds collected by school C is ₹7875.
  3. {tex}\begin{array}{l}New\,\,\,price\,\,matrix\,\,Q = 20\% \times \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{25}\\{100}\\{50}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}\\ \Rightarrow Q = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{25 + 25 \times 0.20}\\{100 + 100 \times 0.20}\\{50 + 50 \times 0.20}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}\\Q = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{30}\\{120}\\{60}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Fans}\\{Mats}\\{Plates}\end{array}\end{array}{/tex}
    New price matrix Q = {tex}20 \% \times\left[\begin{array}{c} 25 \\ 100 \\ 50 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Fans } \\ \text { Mats } \\ \text { Plates } \end{gathered}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 25+25 \times 0.20 \\ 100+100 \times 0.20 \\ 50+50 \times 0.20 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} &\text { Fans } \\ &\text { Mats } \\ &\text { Plates } \end{aligned}{/tex}
    Q = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 30 \\ 120 \\ 60 \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} &\text { Fans } \\ &\text { Mats } \\ &\text { Plates } \end{aligned}{/tex}
    OR
    PQ = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 40 & 50 & 20 \\ 25 & 40 & 30 \\ 35 & 50 & 40 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 30 \\ 120 \\ 60 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    PQ = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1200+6000+1200 \\ 750+4800+1800 \\ 1050+6000+2400 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 8400 \\ 7350 \\ 9450 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Total fund collected = 8400 + 7350 + 9450 = ₹25,200

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
On her birthday, Shanti decided to donate some money to children of an orphanage home. If there were 8 children less, everyone would have got ₹ 10 more. However, if there were 16 children more, everyone would have got ₹ 10 less. Let the number of children be x and the amount distributed by Shanti for one child be y (in ₹).

  1. Find the equations related to the given problem in terms of x and y. (1)
  2. Find the number of children. How much amount is given to each child by Shanti? (1)
  3. Write the equations in form of matrix representation for the information given above? (2)
    OR
    How much amount Shanti spends in distributing the money to all the students of the Orphanage? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: On her birthday, Shanti decided to donate some money to children of an orphanage home. If there were 8 children less, everyone would have got ₹ 10 more. However, if there were 16 children more, everyone would have got ₹ 10 less. Let the number of children be x and the amount distributed by Shanti for one child be y (in ₹). Find the equations related to the given problem in terms of x and y. (1) Find the number of children. How much amount is given to each child by Shanti? (1) Write the equations in form of matrix representation for the information given above? (2) OR How much amount Shanti spends in distributing the money to all the students of the Orphanage? (2)
explanation: Let number of children = x Amount distributed by Shanti for one child = ₹ y Now, Total money = xy and Total money will remain the same. Given that, if there were 8 children less, everyone would have got ₹ 10 more. Total money now = Total money before (x - 8) {tex}\times{/tex} (y + 10) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(y + 10) - 8(y + 10) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy + 10x - 8y - 80 = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x - 8y - 80 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x - 8y = 80 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x - 4y = 40 Also, if there were 16 children more, everyone would have got ₹ 10 less. Total money now = Total money before (x + 16) {tex}\times{/tex} (y - 10) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(y - 10) + 16(y - 10) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 10x + 16y - 160 = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -10x + 16y - 160 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x - 16y + 160 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x - 8y = -80 Thus, required equations are: 5x - 4y = 40 ...(i) 5x - 8y = - 80 ...(ii) On solving eqs. (i) & (ii), we get x = 32 and y = 30. Hence, the number of children = 32 The amount is given to each child by Shanti = Rs. 30. Writing eq. (i) & eq. (ii) in matrix form, we get {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & -4 \\ 5 & -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} OR Total amount = xy = 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = ₹ 960.
solution: Let number of children = x Amount distributed by Shanti for one child = ₹ y Now, Total money = xy and Total money will remain the same. Given that, if there were 8 children less, everyone would have got ₹ 10 more. Total money now = Total money before (x - 8) {tex}\times{/tex} (y + 10) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(y + 10) - 8(y + 10) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy + 10x - 8y - 80 = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x - 8y - 80 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x - 8y = 80 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x - 4y = 40 Also, if there were 16 children more, everyone would have got ₹ 10 less. Total money now = Total money before (x + 16) {tex}\times{/tex} (y - 10) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(y - 10) + 16(y - 10) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 10x + 16y - 160 = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -10x + 16y - 160 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x - 16y + 160 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x - 8y = -80 Thus, required equations are: 5x - 4y = 40 ...(i) 5x - 8y = - 80 ...(ii) On solving eqs. (i) & (ii), we get x = 32 and y = 30. Hence, the number of children = 32 The amount is given to each child by Shanti = Rs. 30. Writing eq. (i) & eq. (ii) in matrix form, we get {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & -4 \\ 5 & -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex} OR Total amount = xy = 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = ₹ 960.
solution_html:
1. Let number of children = x
    Amount distributed by Shanti for one child = ₹ y
    Now, Total money = xy
    and Total money will remain the same.
    Given that, if there were 8 children less, everyone would have got ₹ 10 more.
    Total money now = Total money before
    (x - 8) {tex}\times{/tex} (y + 10) = xy
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(y + 10) - 8(y + 10) = xy
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy + 10x - 8y - 80 = xy
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x - 8y - 80 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x - 8y = 80
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x - 4y = 40
    Also, if there were 16 children more, everyone would have got ₹ 10 less.
    Total money now = Total money before
    (x + 16) {tex}\times{/tex} (y - 10) = xy
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(y - 10) + 16(y - 10) = xy
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 10x + 16y - 160 = xy
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -10x + 16y - 160 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x - 16y + 160 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x - 8y = -80
    Thus, required equations are:
    5x - 4y = 40 ...(i)
    5x - 8y = - 80 ...(ii)

  2. On solving eqs. (i) & (ii), we get x = 32 and y = 30.
    Hence, the number of children = 32
    The amount is given to each child by Shanti = Rs. 30.

  3. Writing eq. (i) & eq. (ii) in matrix form, we get
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & -4 \\ 5 & -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex}{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{c} 40 \\ -80 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    OR
    Total amount = xy = 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = ₹ 960.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
If A = [aij]m {tex}\times{/tex} n and B = [bij]m {tex}\times{/tex} n are two matrices, then A {tex}\pm{/tex} B is of order m {tex}\times{/tex} n and is defined as (A {tex}\pm{/tex} B)ij = aij {tex}\pm{/tex} bij, where i = 1, 2, ..., m and j = 1, 2, ..., n
If A = [aij]m {tex}\times{/tex} n and B = [aij]m {tex}\times{/tex} p are two matrices, then AB is of order m {tex}\times{/tex} p and is defined as (AB)ik = {tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{n}{/tex} air brk = a1kb1k + ai2b2k + ... + ainbnk
Consider A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and D = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]{/tex}

  1. What is the product of matrices A and B? (1)
  2. What are the values of 'a' and 'c' in matrix D such that the equation CD - AB = 0 holds? (1)
  3. What are the values of 'b' and 'd' in matrix D such that the equation CD - AB = 0 holds? (2)
    OR
    What is the sum of matrix A and matrix D? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: If A = [a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n and B = [b ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n are two matrices, then A {tex}\pm{/tex} B is of order m {tex}\times{/tex} n and is defined as (A {tex}\pm{/tex} B) ij = a ij {tex}\pm{/tex} b ij , where i = 1, 2, ..., m and j = 1, 2, ..., n If A = [a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n and B = [a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} p are two matrices, then AB is of order m {tex}\times{/tex} p and is defined as (AB) ik = {tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{n}{/tex} a ir b r k = a 1k b 1k + a i2 b 2k + ... + a in b nk Consider A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and D = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]{/tex} What is the product of matrices A and B? (1) What are the values of 'a' and 'c' in matrix D such that the equation CD - AB = 0 holds? (1) What are the values of 'b' and 'd' in matrix D such that the equation CD - AB = 0 holds? (2) OR What is the sum of matrix A and matrix D? (2)
explanation: AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 10-7 & 4-4 \\ 15+28 & 6+16 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We have, CD - AB = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 8 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c & 3 b+8 d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c-3 & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c-43 & 3 b+8 d-22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} By equality of matrices, we get 2a + 5c - 3 = 0 ...(i) 3a + 8c - 43 = 0 ...(ii) 2b + 5d = 0 ...(iii) 3b + 8d - 22 = 0 ...(iv) Solving (i) and (ii), we get a = -191, c = 77 We have, CD - AB = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 8 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c & 3 b+8 d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c-3 & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c-43 & 3 b+8 d-22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} By equality of matrices, we get 2a + 5c - 3 = 0 ...(i) 3a + 8c - 43 = 0 ...(ii) 2b + 5d = 0 ...(iii) 3b + 8d - 22 = 0 ...(iv) Solving (iii) and (iv), we get b = -110, d = 44 OR We have, B + D = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} -191 & -110 \\ 77 & 44 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -186 & -108\\ 84 & 48 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 10-7 & 4-4 \\ 15+28 & 6+16 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We have, CD - AB = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 8 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c & 3 b+8 d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c-3 & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c-43 & 3 b+8 d-22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} By equality of matrices, we get 2a + 5c - 3 = 0 ...(i) 3a + 8c - 43 = 0 ...(ii) 2b + 5d = 0 ...(iii) 3b + 8d - 22 = 0 ...(iv) Solving (i) and (ii), we get a = -191, c = 77 We have, CD - AB = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 8 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c & 3 b+8 d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c-3 & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c-43 & 3 b+8 d-22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} By equality of matrices, we get 2a + 5c - 3 = 0 ...(i) 3a + 8c - 43 = 0 ...(ii) 2b + 5d = 0 ...(iii) 3b + 8d - 22 = 0 ...(iv) Solving (iii) and (iv), we get b = -110, d = 44 OR We have, B + D = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} -191 & -110 \\ 77 & 44 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -186 & -108\\ 84 & 48 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
1. AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 10-7 & 4-4 \\ 15+28 & 6+16 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

  2. We have, CD - AB = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 8 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c & 3 b+8 d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c-3 & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c-43 & 3 b+8 d-22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    By equality of matrices, we get 2a + 5c - 3 = 0 ...(i)
    3a + 8c - 43 = 0 ...(ii)
    2b + 5d = 0 ...(iii)
    3b + 8d - 22 = 0 ...(iv)
    Solving (i) and (ii), we get a = -191, c = 77

  3. We have, CD - AB = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 8 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c & 3 b+8 d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c-3 & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c-43 & 3 b+8 d-22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    By equality of matrices, we get 2a + 5c - 3 = 0 ...(i)
    3a + 8c - 43 = 0 ...(ii)
    2b + 5d = 0 ...(iii)
    3b + 8d - 22 = 0 ...(iv)
    Solving (iii) and (iv), we get b = -110, d = 44
    OR
    We have, B + D = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} -191 & -110 \\ 77 & 44 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -186 & -108\\ 84 & 48 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
The nut and bolt manufacturing business has gained popularity due to the rapid Industrialization and introduction of the Capital-Intensive Techniques in the Industries that are used as the Industrial fasteners to connect various machines and structures. Mr. Suresh is in Manufacturing business of Nuts and bolts. He produces three types of bolts, x, y, and z which he sells in two markets. Annual sales (in ₹) indicated below:
Markets Products   x y z I 10000 2000 18000 II 6000 20000 8000

  1. If unit sales prices of x, y and z are ₹2.50, ₹1.50 and ₹1.00 respectively, then find the total revenue collected from Market-I &II. (1)
  2. If the unit costs of the above three commodities are ₹2.00, ₹1.00 and 50 paise respectively, then find the cost price in Market I and Market II. (1)
  3. If the unit costs of the above three commodities are ₹2.00, ₹1.00 and 50 paise respectively, then find gross profit from both the markets. (2)
    OR
    If matrix A = {tex}\left[a_{i j}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} where aij = 1, if i {tex}\ne{/tex} j and aij = 0, if i = j then find A2. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: The nut and bolt manufacturing business has gained popularity due to the rapid Industrialization and introduction of the Capital-Intensive Techniques in the Industries that are used as the Industrial fasteners to connect various machines and structures. Mr. Suresh is in Manufacturing business of Nuts and bolts. He produces three types of bolts, x, y, and z which he sells in two markets. Annual sales (in ₹) indicated below: Markets Products x y z I 10000 2000 18000 II 6000 20000 8000 If unit sales prices of x, y and z are ₹2.50, ₹1.50 and ₹1.00 respectively, then find the total revenue collected from Market-I &II. (1) If the unit costs of the above three commodities are ₹2.00, ₹1.00 and 50 paise respectively, then find the cost price in Market I and Market II. (1) If the unit costs of the above three commodities are ₹2.00, ₹1.00 and 50 paise respectively, then find gross profit from both the markets. (2) OR If matrix A = {tex}\left[a_{i j}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} where a ij = 1, if i {tex}\ne{/tex} j and a ij = 0, if i = j then find A 2 . (2)
explanation: Let A be the 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix representing the annual sales of products in two markets. {tex}\begin{array}{l}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,y\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,z\\\,A = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{10000}&{2000}&{18000}\\{6000}&{20000}&{8000}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Market\,\,I}\\{Market\,\,II}\end{array}\end{array}{/tex} Now, revenue = sale price {tex}\times{/tex} number of items sold AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 25000+3000+18000 \\ 15000+30000+8000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, the revenue collected from Market I = ₹46000 and the revenue collected from Market II = ₹53000. Let C be the column matrix representing cost price of each unit of products x, y, z. {tex}C = \,\left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}2\\1\\{0.5}\end{array}} \right]{/tex} AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0.5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 20000+2000+9000 \\ 12000+20000+4000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Cost price in Market I is ₹31000 and in Market is ₹36000. Now, Profit matrix = Revenue matrix - Cost matrix {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB - AC {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 15000 \\ 17000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, the gross profit from both the markets = ₹15000 + ₹17000 = ₹32000 OR A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0+1 & 0+0 \\ 0+0 & 0+1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 2 = I
solution: Let A be the 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix representing the annual sales of products in two markets. {tex}\begin{array}{l}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,y\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,z\\\,A = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{10000}&{2000}&{18000}\\{6000}&{20000}&{8000}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Market\,\,I}\\{Market\,\,II}\end{array}\end{array}{/tex} Now, revenue = sale price {tex}\times{/tex} number of items sold AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 25000+3000+18000 \\ 15000+30000+8000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, the revenue collected from Market I = ₹46000 and the revenue collected from Market II = ₹53000. Let C be the column matrix representing cost price of each unit of products x, y, z. {tex}C = \,\left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}2\\1\\{0.5}\end{array}} \right]{/tex} AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0.5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 20000+2000+9000 \\ 12000+20000+4000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Cost price in Market I is ₹31000 and in Market is ₹36000. Now, Profit matrix = Revenue matrix - Cost matrix {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB - AC {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 15000 \\ 17000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, the gross profit from both the markets = ₹15000 + ₹17000 = ₹32000 OR A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0+1 & 0+0 \\ 0+0 & 0+1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 2 = I
solution_html:
1. Let A be the 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix representing the annual sales of products in two markets. {tex}\begin{array}{l}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,y\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,z\\\,A = \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{10000}&{2000}&{18000}\\{6000}&{20000}&{8000}\end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}{Market\,\,I}\\{Market\,\,II}\end{array}\end{array}{/tex}
    Now, revenue = sale price {tex}\times{/tex} number of items sold
    AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 25000+3000+18000 \\ 15000+30000+8000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Therefore, the revenue collected from Market I = ₹46000 and the revenue collected from Market II = ₹53000.
  2. Let C be the column matrix representing cost price of each unit of products x, y, z.
    {tex}C = \,\left[ {\begin{array}{ccccccccccccccc}2\\1\\{0.5}\end{array}} \right]{/tex}
    AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10000 & 2000 & 18000 \\ 6000 & 20000 & 8000 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0.5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 20000+2000+9000 \\ 12000+20000+4000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
    Cost price in Market I is ₹31000 and in Market is ₹36000.
  3. Now, Profit matrix = Revenue matrix - Cost matrix
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB - AC
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 46000 \\ 53000 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{l} 31000 \\ 36000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 15000 \\ 17000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Therefore, the gross profit from both the markets = ₹15000 + ₹17000 = ₹32000
    OR
    A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    A2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0+1 & 0+0 \\ 0+0 & 0+1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A2 = I

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Three students, Neha, Rani and Sam go to a market to purchase stationery items. Neha buys 4 pens, 3 notepads and 2 erasers and pays ₹ 60. Rani buys 2 pens, 4 notepads and 6 erasers for ₹ 90. Sam pays ₹ 70 for 6 pens, 2 notepads and 3 erasers.
Based upon the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Form the equations required to solve the problem of finding the price of each item, and express it in the matrix form {tex}{AX}={B}{/tex}. (1)
  2. Find {tex}|{A}|{/tex} and confirm if it is possible to find {tex}{A}^{-1}{/tex}. (1)
    1. Find {tex}{A}^{-1}{/tex}, if possible, and write the formula to find X. (2)

      OR

    2. Find {tex}{A}^{2}-8 {I}{/tex}, where I is an identity matrix. (2)
question_text: Three students, Neha, Rani and Sam go to a market to purchase stationery items. Neha buys 4 pens, 3 notepads and 2 erasers and pays ₹ 60. Rani buys 2 pens, 4 notepads and 6 erasers for ₹ 90. Sam pays ₹ 70 for 6 pens, 2 notepads and 3 erasers. Based upon the above information, answer the following questions: Form the equations required to solve the problem of finding the price of each item, and express it in the matrix form {tex}{AX}={B}{/tex} . (1) Find {tex}|{A}|{/tex} and confirm if it is possible to find {tex}{A}^{-1}{/tex} . (1) Find {tex}{A}^{-1}{/tex} , if possible, and write the formula to find X. (2) OR Find {tex}{A}^{2}-8 {I}{/tex} , where I is an identity matrix. (2)
explanation: Let the price of each pen, notepad, eraser be ₹ {tex}x{/tex} , ₹ {tex}y{/tex} and ₹ {tex}z{/tex} respectively Given system in the form {tex}{AX}={B}{/tex} is {tex}\left(\begin{array}{lll}4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l}x \\ y \\ z\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{l}60 \\ 90 \\ 70\end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}|{A}|=50 \neq 0{/tex} , hence {tex}{A}^{-1}{/tex} exists {tex}{A}^{-1}=\frac{\operatorname{adj} {A}}{|{A}|}=\frac{1}{50}\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & -5 & 10 \\ 30 & 0 & -20 \\ -20 & 10 & 10\end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}{X}={A}^{-1} {~B}{/tex} OR {tex}A^{2}=\left(\begin{array}{lll}4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{lll}4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll}34 & 28 & 32 \\ 52 & 34 & 46 \\ 46 & 32 & 33\end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}A^{2}-8 I=\left(\begin{array}{lll}26 & 28 & 32 \\ 52 & 26 & 46 \\ 46 & 32 & 2.5\end{array}\right){/tex}
solution: Let the price of each pen, notepad, eraser be ₹ {tex}x{/tex} , ₹ {tex}y{/tex} and ₹ {tex}z{/tex} respectively Given system in the form {tex}{AX}={B}{/tex} is {tex}\left(\begin{array}{lll}4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l}x \\ y \\ z\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{l}60 \\ 90 \\ 70\end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}|{A}|=50 \neq 0{/tex} , hence {tex}{A}^{-1}{/tex} exists {tex}{A}^{-1}=\frac{\operatorname{adj} {A}}{|{A}|}=\frac{1}{50}\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & -5 & 10 \\ 30 & 0 & -20 \\ -20 & 10 & 10\end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}{X}={A}^{-1} {~B}{/tex} OR {tex}A^{2}=\left(\begin{array}{lll}4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{lll}4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll}34 & 28 & 32 \\ 52 & 34 & 46 \\ 46 & 32 & 33\end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}A^{2}-8 I=\left(\begin{array}{lll}26 & 28 & 32 \\ 52 & 26 & 46 \\ 46 & 32 & 2.5\end{array}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let the price of each pen, notepad, eraser be ₹ {tex}x{/tex}, ₹ {tex}y{/tex} and ₹ {tex}z{/tex} respectively
    Given system in the form {tex}{AX}={B}{/tex} is {tex}\left(\begin{array}{lll}4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l}x \\ y \\ z\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{l}60 \\ 90 \\ 70\end{array}\right){/tex}
  2. {tex}|{A}|=50 \neq 0{/tex}, hence {tex}{A}^{-1}{/tex} exists
    1. {tex}{A}^{-1}=\frac{\operatorname{adj} {A}}{|{A}|}=\frac{1}{50}\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & -5 & 10 \\ 30 & 0 & -20 \\ -20 & 10 & 10\end{array}\right){/tex}
      {tex}{X}={A}^{-1} {~B}{/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}A^{2}=\left(\begin{array}{lll}4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{lll}4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll}34 & 28 & 32 \\ 52 & 34 & 46 \\ 46 & 32 & 33\end{array}\right){/tex}
      {tex}A^{2}-8 I=\left(\begin{array}{lll}26 & 28 & 32 \\ 52 & 26 & 46 \\ 46 & 32 & 2.5\end{array}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A scholarship is a sum of money provided to a student to help him or her pay for education. Some students are granted scholarships based on their academic achievements, while others are rewarded based on their financial needs.

Every year a school offers scholarships to girl children and meritorious achievers based on certain criteria. In the session 2022-23, the school offered monthly scholarship of ₹ 3,000 each to some girl students and ₹ 4,000 each to meritorious achievers in academics as well as sports.
In all, 50 students were given the scholarships and monthly expenditure incurred by the school on scholarships was {tex}₹ 1,80,000{/tex}.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Express the given information algebraically using matrices. (1)
  2. Check whether the system of matrix equations so obtained is consistent or not. (1)
    1. Find the number of scholarships of each kind given by the school, using matrices. (2)

      OR

    2. Had the amount of scholarship given to each girl child and meritorious student been interchanged, what would be the monthly expenditure incurred by the school? (2)
question_text: A scholarship is a sum of money provided to a student to help him or her pay for education. Some students are granted scholarships based on their academic achievements, while others are rewarded based on their financial needs. Every year a school offers scholarships to girl children and meritorious achievers based on certain criteria. In the session 2022-23, the school offered monthly scholarship of ₹ 3,000 each to some girl students and ₹ 4,000 each to meritorious achievers in academics as well as sports. In all, 50 students were given the scholarships and monthly expenditure incurred by the school on scholarships was {tex}₹ 1,80,000{/tex} . Based on the above information, answer the following questions: Express the given information algebraically using matrices. (1) Check whether the system of matrix equations so obtained is consistent or not. (1) Find the number of scholarships of each kind given by the school, using matrices. (2) OR Had the amount of scholarship given to each girl child and meritorious student been interchanged, what would be the monthly expenditure incurred by the school? (2)
explanation: Let No. of girl child scholarships {tex}={x}{/tex} No. of meritorious achievers {tex}=y{/tex} {tex} x+y=50 {/tex} {tex} 3000 x+4000 y=180000 \text { or } 3 x+4 y=180 {/tex} {tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]} {/tex} {tex} \left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|=1 \neq 0 {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} system is consistent. {tex} \text { Let } {A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right], {X}=\left[\begin{array}{l} {x} \\ {y} \end{array}\right], {B}=\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} {AX}={B} \Rightarrow {X}={A}^{-1} {~B} {/tex} {tex} {X}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 4 & -1 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 20 \\ 30 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {x}=20, {y}=30 {/tex} OR {tex} \text { Required expenditure } {/tex} {tex}=₹[30(3000)+20(4000)] {/tex} {tex} =₹ 1,70,000 {/tex}
solution: Let No. of girl child scholarships {tex}={x}{/tex} No. of meritorious achievers {tex}=y{/tex} {tex} x+y=50 {/tex} {tex} 3000 x+4000 y=180000 \text { or } 3 x+4 y=180 {/tex} {tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]} {/tex} {tex} \left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|=1 \neq 0 {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} system is consistent. {tex} \text { Let } {A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right], {X}=\left[\begin{array}{l} {x} \\ {y} \end{array}\right], {B}=\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} {AX}={B} \Rightarrow {X}={A}^{-1} {~B} {/tex} {tex} {X}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 4 & -1 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 20 \\ 30 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {x}=20, {y}=30 {/tex} OR {tex} \text { Required expenditure } {/tex} {tex}=₹[30(3000)+20(4000)] {/tex} {tex} =₹ 1,70,000 {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let No. of girl child scholarships {tex}={x}{/tex}
    No. of meritorious achievers {tex}=y{/tex}
    {tex} x+y=50 {/tex}
    {tex} 3000 x+4000 y=180000 \text { or } 3 x+4 y=180 {/tex}
    {tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]} {/tex}
  2. {tex} \left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|=1 \neq 0 {/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} system is consistent.
    1. {tex} \text { Let } {A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right], {X}=\left[\begin{array}{l} {x} \\ {y} \end{array}\right], {B}=\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
      {tex} {AX}={B} \Rightarrow {X}={A}^{-1} {~B} {/tex}
      {tex} {X}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 4 & -1 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 20 \\ 30 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
      {tex} \Rightarrow {x}=20, {y}=30 {/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex} \text { Required expenditure } {/tex} {tex}=₹[30(3000)+20(4000)] {/tex}
      {tex} =₹ 1,70,000 {/tex}

question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, prove that A3 - 6A2 + 7A + 2I = 0.
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , prove that A 3 - 6A 2 + 7A + 2I = 0.
solution_html:
L.H.S. = A3 - 6A2 + 7A + 2I
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} - 6\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right] + 7\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} + 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 0 + 4}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{2 + 0 + 6} \\ {0 + 0 + 2}&{0 + 4 + 0}&{0 + 2 + 3} \\ {2 + 0 + 6}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{4 + 0 + 9} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} - 6\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 0 + 4}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{2 + 0 + 6} \\ {0 + 0 + 2}&{0 + 4 + 0}&{0 + 2 + 3} \\ {2 + 0 + 6}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{4 + 0 + 9} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0&{14} \\ 0&{14}&7 \\ {14}&0&{21} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&0 \\ 0&2&0 \\ 0&0&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {5 + 0 + 16}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{10 + 0 + 24} \\ {2 + 0 + 10}&{0 + 8 + 0}&{4 + 4 + 15} \\ {8 + 0 + 26}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{16 + 0 + 39} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {30}&0&{48} \\ {12}&{24}&{30} \\ {48}&0&{78} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {7 + 2}&{0 + 0}&{14 + 0} \\ {0 + 0}&{14 + 2}&{7 + 0} \\ {14 + 0}&{0 + 0}&{21 + 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {21}&0&{34} \\ {12}&8&{23} \\ {34}&0&{55} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {30}&0&{48} \\ {12}&{24}&{30} \\ {48}&0&{78} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&0&{14} \\ 0&{16}&7 \\ {14}&0&{23} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {21 - 30}&{0 - 0}&{34 - 48} \\ {12 - 12}&{8 - 24}&{23 - 30} \\ {34 - 48}&{0 - 0}&{55 - 78} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&0&{14} \\ 0&{16}&7 \\ {14}&0&{23} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9}&0&{ - 14} \\ 0&{ - 16}&{ - 7} \\ { - 14}&0&{ - 23} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&0&{14} \\ 0&{16}&7 \\ {14}&0&{23} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9 + 9}&{0 + 0}&{ - 14 + 14} \\ {0 + 0}&{ - 16 + 16}&{ - 7 + 7} \\ { - 14 + 14}&{0 + 0}&{ - 23 + 23} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} (Zero matrix)
= R.H.S. Proved.

question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and I is the identity matrix of order 2, show that {tex}I + A = (I - A)\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .

\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}I - A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{ - \sin \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \\ {\frac{{\sin \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{\cos \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{ - \sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \\ {\frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} L.H.S. = R.H.S. Proved.
